places.hours_raw: Tue 13:00–17:00, Wed–Sat 10:00–17:00 (last order 16:30), closed Sun & Mon
places.cuisine: House-roasted coffee, homemade cakes and baked sweets
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hitoshi Hirano spent his student years traveling roughly 50 countries across Central and South America, Asia, and Europe, developing a deep love of coffee culture along the way. He established a guesthouse in Sapporo in 2008 and then opened a book café there in 2010. Beginning in

chef_bio.origin: 長野市立町 / 善光寺門前
chef_bio.background: Hitoshi Hirano spent his student years traveling roughly 50 countries across Central and South America, Asia, and Europe, developing a deep love of coffee culture along the way. He established a guesthouse in Sapporo in 2008 and then opened a book café there in 2010. Beginning in 2013 he taught himself coffee roasting, and in 2016 he relocated to Nagano and opened 平野珈琲 on New Year's Eve — renovating the roughly 100-year-old machiya alongside friends. In 2019 he earned his CQI Q Arabica Grader certification (an internationally recognised coffee quality credential).
chef_bio.philosophy: Hirano sources beans by building direct relationships with trustworthy producers worldwide, visiting farms when possible to understand each grower's practices. He roasts to bring out the unique character of each bean. The baking team uses simple techniques, adjusts recipes seasonally, and refines flavors continuously — the guiding idea being restraint and harmony with coffee rather than dessert showmanship.
chef_bio.specialty: 自家焙煎コーヒー (self-roasted specialty coffee) — 14–15 varieties of single-origins and blends roasted almost daily on a Fuji Royal machine, alongside seasonally changing handmade cakes and pastries designed to pair harmoniously with the coffee.

anecdotes.text: Hirano and friends renovated the 100-year-old wooden machiya themselves, opening on New Year's Eve 2016. The second floor café retains salvaged materials — gym floor planks, church and school chairs — and vintage speakers playing jazz records. Antique clocks, retro cameras, and mechanical objects fill the shelves, and reviewers consistently note that 'time seems to stand still' in the space.
In 2019 Hirano passed the CQI Q Arabica Grader exam, adding an internationally recognised professional credential to what had been a self-taught roasting practice.

The shop has since announced a second location — 平野珈琲 山の喫茶室 (Mountain Tea Room) — opening in the Iizuna highlands roughly 20 minutes from the main shop, suggesting the brand is quietly expanding while keeping its handcrafted ethos.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available on cross-contamination controls. The kitchen produces flour-based cakes and pastries daily; no allergen separation protocols have been reported in any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce does not appear in the menu at all — this is a coffee and cake shop with no savory cooking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All baked goods appear to contain dairy; no plant-based alternatives have been mentioned. Cross-contact between dairy-containing pastries and any theoretical vegan order cannot be ruled out.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling protocols or allergen menus have been reported by any source. The shop is a small two-person operation; direct inquiry by phone (050-3699-7897) or via Instagram (@hirano_coffee) before visiting is strongly recommended for anyone with serious dietary restrictions.

positives.text: Plain black coffee (all varieties) is naturally gluten-free and vegan — the shop stocks 14–15 bean varieties and roasts almost daily, so coffee-only visits are low-risk.

cultural_comfort.note: Small owner-run café with no English signage or menu reported; the intimate, quiet atmosphere is welcoming but Japanese-language navigation is expected.

places.name: フフレ (Fufure)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu. Standard French kitchen: house-made bread served at every meal, cream sauces may contain flour as a thickener, and soy sauce (wheat-based) appears in vinaigrette preparations (e.g., confirmed soy-sherry vinaigrette on the menu). Cross-contamination risk is unquantified. One source confirms allergy requests can be communicated in advance for menu adjustments. Celiac diners should call ahead and ask specifically about flour/shoyu in each course component.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu. The kitchen leans heavily on seasonal Shinshu vegetables, but courses use butter, cream, meat, and fish throughout. One source noted that dietary restrictions can be accommodated if communicated in advance, so vegan adjustments may be possible on request — ask when booking.
places.hours_raw: Mon & Thu–Sun: 11:30–14:00, 18:00–19:30 (last order) / Tue: 11:30–14:00 only / Wed: closed / also closed the 1st & 3rd Tuesdays each month
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Fufure (formerly fufule Kitchen) opened in 2014 near Zenkoji Temple in Nagano City and has been run by a two-person team managing both cooking and front-of-house. The name change from フフレキッチン to フフレ appears on Tabelog as a rebranding, with the restaurant continuing at the same ad

chef_bio.origin: 長野市 / Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture
chef_bio.background: Fufure (formerly fufule Kitchen) opened in 2014 near Zenkoji Temple in Nagano City and has been run by a two-person team managing both cooking and front-of-house.
chef_bio.philosophy: Casual, unpretentious French dining in a relaxed setting — 料理も内装もオシャレだけど気取りがない (sophisticated food and decor, without pretension). The kitchen prioritizes fresh local produce and lets seasonal ingredients speak for themselves.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal prefecture-style French prefix courses built around Shinshu (Nagano) vegetables — roughly 85% of ingredients are locally sourced; the rotating prix-fixe menu changes every two months.

anecdotes.text: Opened in 2014 near Zenkoji and built a loyal following among both local residents and visitors from across Nagano Prefecture.
Menu rotates every two months to track Shinshu seasons, with over 80-85% of ingredients sourced from within Nagano Prefecture.
The two-person operation — one cooking, one serving — maintains consistent hospitality described as friendly and warm throughout the meal.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen or protocols mentioned across any reviewed source. Standard French kitchen with house-made bread at every service.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce confirmed in at least one vinaigrette preparation (soy and sherry vinegar vinaigrette on seared tuna appetizer), indicating standard wheat-containing shoyu is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy (cream, cheese, butter) and animal proteins are core to most course components; no vegan-separated prep line noted.

staff_allergy_handling.text: One source confirms that if dietary restrictions or food allergies are communicated in advance, the kitchen will adjust individual dish contents accordingly.

positives.text: Vegetable-forward philosophy with over 85% locally sourced Shinshu produce means many course components are plant-based at their core.
Small two-person operation makes customization more feasible than at a large restaurant — direct communication with the kitchen is straightforward.

cultural_comfort.note: Small neighborhood French bistro run by two people; comfortable for Japanese-speaking diners, limited English likely, but a welcoming and unpretentious atmosphere.

places.name: 古民家dining BAR GOFUKU (Kominka Dining BAR GOFUKU)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options are listed or advertised anywhere. The menu includes wheat-containing items throughout: soy sauce is standard in the kitchen, beer is a core offering, and pasta and quiche appear on the lunch menu. The restaurant has noted it can accommodate allergy requests when notified in advance, but there is no dedicated fryer or GF protocol confirmed. Cross-contamination risk is significant in this open kitchen dining-bar format. Celiac diners should call ahead and verify specific dishes.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu. Standard dining-bar offerings include rotisserie chicken, jerk chicken, garlic shrimp, and butter chicken curry. No plant-based dishes are advertised. Dashi, butter, and meat are central to the menu. Guests with strict requirements should contact the restaurant in advance.
places.hours_raw: Wed–Sat and days before/after public holidays 11:30–15:00 (last order 14:30), 18:00–22:00 (last order 21:30) / Sun & holidays 11:30–15:00 (last order 14:30) only / closed Mon & Tue
places.cuisine: Dining bar, rotisserie chicken, local craft beer
places.notes: Opened in April 2015 inside a 100-year-old former kimono wholesaler's building (呉服店) in Nagano's Gondo entertainment district. The name GOFUKU is a double pun: 呉服 (gofuku) means traditional kimono fabric, nodding to the building's past, while 五福 (gofuku) means five blessings. The

chef_bio.origin: Gondo-cho, Nagano City — the historic entertainment district one stop from central Nagano on the Nagaden line.
chef_bio.background: Opened in April 2015 inside a 100-year-old former kimono wholesaler's building (呉服店) in Nagano's Gondo entertainment district. The name GOFUKU is a double pun: 呉服 (gofuku) means traditional kimono fabric, nodding to the building's past, while 五福 (gofuku) means five blessings. The complex was extensively renovated to reveal its Meiji-era wooden structure while introducing contemporary design elements. Beyond the restaurant itself, the property houses a patisserie, hair salon, nail salon, vintage clothing shop, and shared office spaces, functioning as a community hub.
chef_bio.philosophy: Manager Tokumaaru Emi, who has run the restaurant since opening, describes it as a place where the warmth comes not just from food and space but from the relationships between guests, staff, suppliers, and neighboring businesses. The goal is an atmosphere people return to.
chef_bio.specialty: Rotisserie chicken marinated in house-made spices and slow-roasted whole in a dedicated oven — crispy outside, juicy inside — served alongside a rotating selection of around 25 local Nagano craft beers.

anecdotes.text: The building is not just a restaurant but a shared public space: alongside GOFUKU, a patisserie, hair salon, nail salon, vintage clothing shop, and shared offices all occupy the renovated 100-year-old compound, making it a small neighborhood of its own inside Gondo.
The rotisserie oven renders excess fat while keeping the chicken moist — the kitchen's signature investment and the dish that defines the menu.
During summer 2025 exterior renovation work, the terrace was temporarily closed, showing the restaurant's commitment to ongoing upkeep of the historic structure.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free preparation area or protocol is mentioned anywhere. This is a full dining-bar kitchen handling pasta, quiche, beer-battered or marinated meats, and wheat soy sauce as standard ingredients.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy sauce (wheat-containing) is assumed to be used throughout the kitchen; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned on any menu or review source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Rotisserie chicken, jerk chicken, and garlic shrimp are core menu items; butter and dairy appear in lunch sets. No separation of vegan prep is indicated.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Search results indicate the restaurant can accommodate food allergies when notified in advance, but no formal allergy menu or written protocol has been confirmed.

positives.text: English menu available, lowering the communication barrier for allergy discussions with staff.

cultural_comfort.note: English menu available; online reservations accepted via HotPepper; staff described as welcoming and attentive to guests' needs.

places.name: 肉の店 牛見 (Niku no Mise Ushimi)
places.gf_detail: The signature beef cutlet (zabuton) is breaded with wheat panko and fried in a shared fryer. Sauces (balsamic, ginger, house dressing) have unknown ingredients — wheat-based soy sauce is common in such preparations. The shop is a traditional butcher/deli with no gluten-free menu or allergen guidance published. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-specialist butcher and Western lunch spot — the entire menu centers on beef, pork, and chicken. There are no plant-based mains. Croquettes and sides may contain meat or animal-based fillings. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–18:30 (Tabelog); lunch service 11:30–14:00 LO. Closed Sundays and on the 1st of each month.
places.neighborhood: 善光寺下 / 東之門町 (Zenkoji Higashi-no-mon-cho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A family-run butcher shop founded in 1948 in the approach lane to Zenkoji Temple's East Gate. The business passed through three generations, with Ishizaka Yasushi — the third-generation owner — later opening a separate Western restaurant called USHIMI洋食店 in the Nitta-machi area i

chef_bio.origin: 長野市長野東之門町 (Higashi-no-mon-cho, Nagano City) — near the eastern approach to Zenkoji Temple
chef_bio.background: A family-run butcher shop founded in 1948 in the approach lane to Zenkoji Temple's East Gate. The business passed through three generations, with Ishizaka Yasushi — the third-generation owner — later opening a separate Western restaurant called USHIMI洋食店 in the Nitta-machi area in 2018. After his father's death in 2022 and the closure of USHIMI洋食店 on March 15, 2023, Yasushi consolidated both businesses into the original butcher shop, reopening on May 9, 2023 as a combined butcher, deli, and Western lunch spot. His stated philosophy: 'To survive the times, we must adapt and try new things. '
chef_bio.philosophy: Artisanal neighborhood butchering focused on quality domestic and Shinshu beef; the lunch counter extends that same beef-first philosophy to plated meals, bringing accessible quality to temple pilgrims and local regulars alike.
chef_bio.specialty: Zabuton beef cutlet (牛カツ定食) — a rare, lightly marbled cut of beef breaded and fried to a pink medium-rare, served with ginger sauce, mustard, and Yawataya Isogoro spice blend, plus salad, soup, and a drink. Also the Ushimi Sandwich (Ushimiサンド) featuring the same beef in a sandwich format.

anecdotes.text: After his father's death in 2022, Yasushi chose to merge his popular Western restaurant into the original family butcher shop rather than close either. He framed it simply: 'To survive the times, we must adapt and try new things.'
The reopening in May 2023 drew immediate excitement from regulars who had mourned the March closure of USHIMI洋食店 — many rushed back for 'that beef cutlet sandwich' they thought was gone for good.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The beef cutlet is breaded in wheat panko and fried; no dedicated fryer for gluten-free items is indicated. All fried items (croquettes, cutlets) are prepared in the same kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sauces including ginger sauce and balsamic house sauce are house-made; no information confirms they are wheat-free. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a meat butcher shop and the deli/lunch counter handles beef, pork, and chicken throughout. No plant-based items are produced in a separate area.

cultural_comfort.note: A working neighborhood butcher shop with a small lunch counter near Zenkoji; primarily Japanese clientele with no English menu or signage noted, but straightforward counter ordering.

places.name: 麺道 麒麟児 大門店 (Mendo Kirinji Daimon-ten)
places.gf_detail: Standard ramen shop with wheat-based noodles throughout. Soy sauce (shoyu) tare almost certainly contains wheat. No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation information found on any source. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: All broths are animal-based: the light soy (中華そば) uses whole chicken, chicken feet (momiji), and duck; the chicken broth (鶏そば) is a rich tori paitan reduced from chicken carcasses. No plant-based or vegan options mentioned on the menu or any source.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 (closes once the soup or noodles run out); irregular days off (often closed Wednesdays, but varies by month)
places.neighborhood: Zenkōji Monzen / inside Patio Daimon, Daimon-chō
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hoshi Hirohito opened the original 麺道 麒麟児 in Nagano in 2011 after roughly 15 years training at well-regarded ramen establishments in the Tsubame-Sanjo region of Niigata. By 2023 he had built enough of a following — with consistent queues at the main shop — to launch the Kirinji G

chef_bio.background: Hoshi Hirohito opened the original 麺道 麒麟児 in Nagano in 2011 after roughly 15 years training at well-regarded ramen establishments in the Tsubame-Sanjo region of Niigata. By 2023 he had built enough of a following — with consistent queues at the main shop — to launch the Kirinji Group multi-location strategy, placing this branch inside the Patio Daimon kurazukuri warehouse complex a short walk from Zenkoji Temple.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hoshi's stated aim for the Daimon branch was to bring Kirinji's ramen to Nagano's tourist heartland: 'Zenkoji is a premier tourist destination — I want to serve our ramen to visitors from outside the prefecture and international tourists. ' The group tagline is '笑顔をつなぐ心温まるおもてなし' (warm hospitality connecting smiles).
chef_bio.specialty: Two-pillar menu: 中華そば (light soy, featuring whole chicken and chicken feet slow-simmered with a touch of duck) and 鶏そば (rich tori paitan reduced from chicken carcasses to a silky, near-porridge consistency). Noodles are house-made to pair with each broth.

anecdotes.text: The Daimon branch opened on April 28, 2023 as the Kirinji Group's first satellite location, deliberately sited inside the renovated kurazukuri (white-walled storehouse) Patio Daimon complex to capture Zenkoji pilgrimage-route foot traffic.
The original main shop had already established a reputation for some of the longest and most consistent queues in Nagano City before the expansion began.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free preparation area or fryer documented. Wheat noodles are the core product; cross-contamination is a near-certainty.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard shoyu tare is used in the 中華そば; Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every broth on the menu is chicken- or duck-based. No plant-based broth or topping alternatives have been documented.

cultural_comfort.note: Tiny counter-focused ramen shop (11 seats) near Zenkoji Temple; no English signage confirmed, though the Patio Daimon complex draws foreign tourists — basic Japanese or pointing at the menu board will suffice.

places.name: パティスリー HEIGORO 本店 (Pâtisserie HEIGORO Honten)
places.gf_detail: All kakigori (shaved ice) flavors confirmed to contain wheat (小麦) alongside dairy and eggs per the café menu allergen listings. Baked goods, cakes, scones, and pastries are all flour-based. No gluten-free menu items or dedicated GF options are offered. Celiac diners should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around dairy (fresh cream, fromage blanc, condensed milk, mascarpone, Jersey milk soft-serve), eggs, and gelatin. Kakigori syrups are paired with dairy creams; cakes and baked goods contain butter, cream, and eggs throughout. No plant-based alternatives are listed.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri 11:00–17:00 (last order 16:00), Sat, Sun & holidays 10:00–17:00 (last order 16:00), closed Wed & Thu. Check the official Instagram (@heigoro_fujiya) for the latest opening days.
places.cuisine: Patisserie & cakes, seasonal shaved ice (kakigōri), café
places.notes: HEIGORO is the patisserie arm of The Fujiya Gohonjin, a Western-style hospitality complex on the Zenkoji omotesando in Nagano. The Fujiya traces its roots to 1648, when it operated as a honjin — an official inn reserved for feudal lords, in this case the Maeda clan of Kaga. The m

chef_bio.origin: 長野市大門町 / Zenkoji omotesando, Nagano
chef_bio.background: HEIGORO is the patisserie arm of The Fujiya Gohonjin, a Western-style hospitality complex on the Zenkoji omotesando in Nagano. The Fujiya traces its roots to 1648, when it operated as a honjin — an official inn reserved for feudal lords, in this case the Maeda clan of Kaga. The modern patisserie occupies a renovated building on the historic approach street, blending the estate's long-standing service culture with classical French confectionery.
chef_bio.philosophy: Classical refinement rooted in heritage — elegant packaging and flavors suited for both gifting and in-house café enjoyment, reflecting the estate's centuries-long tradition of hospitality.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal kakigori (shaved ice) made with Shinshu natural ice and housemade fruit syrups; classical cakes and long-shelf-life baked goods including Cake au Cube (cubic pound cakes with local fruit) and eight-variety sablé assortments.

anecdotes.text: The main store closed in March 2020 and remained shuttered through the pandemic, reopening on March 17, 2022 with a focused lineup of baked goods before gradually restoring fresh pastries and cakes.

The summer kakigori uses fluffy-shaved Shinshu natural ice as its base; the peach edition features housemade Nagano-peach syrup, fromage blanc cream, meringue, and an Earl Grey gelato core — served with a pot of hot tea.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All café kakigori flavors list 小麦 (wheat) as a confirmed allergen. Pastry production shares the same kitchen environment used for wheat-based cakes, tarts, scones, and baked goods — cross-contamination risk is high for any gluten-sensitive diner.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce used in this dessert/patisserie context, but wheat is present throughout via flour-based pastries, sablés, scones, tart shells, and kakigori syrups. Wheat is a confirmed allergen in all tested menu items.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy is foundational to the menu — butter, cream, fromage blanc, condensed milk, Jersey milk soft-serve, and mascarpone appear across all product categories. Egg is also listed as a confirmed allergen in all kakigori items. There is no plant-based section or vegan-friendly preparation track.

staff_allergy_handling.text: One source notes that advance reservations (at least 2 days prior) may allow for some allergy accommodation — contact directly at 026-266-0156 or heigoro@fujiyagohonjin.co.jp to discuss specific needs before visiting.

positives.text: Allergen information is displayed on café menu items (dairy, eggs, wheat, almonds, soy listed per item), allowing diners to identify specific allergens before ordering.

cultural_comfort.note: Located on the main Zenkoji tourist approach and accustomed to foreign visitors, so basic English communication is likely manageable, though the menu and ordering system are primarily in Japanese.

places.name: CHAYADO 百八 善光寺門前 (CHAYADO Momoya Zenkoji Monzen)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or allergen declarations found. Plain matcha drinks and single-ingredient gelato scoops are plausibly wheat-free, but the croquet monsieur (corn bread with filling) contains gluten, and no dedicated fryer or separated preparation is documented. Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) does not appear in the menu, but there is no allergen sheet on record. Celiacs should ask in detail before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Gelato is dairy-based (Kurohime Highland milk is a selling point), matcha lattes are made with milk, and honey is used in some drinks. Some fruit-forward gelato flavors (natsuhaze berry, blueberry, strawberry) may be dairy-free or low-dairy depending on the batch, but no vegan labeling or dedicated vegan options are declared anywhere on the menu. Ask staff about specific daily flavors.
places.hours_raw: 9:00–18:30 (LO 18:00); open year-round with no set closed days
places.cuisine: Japanese tea café, gelato, modern Japanese style
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] CHAYADO 百八 opened on December 9, 2025 in the Higashigoshomachi district along Nagano's central shopping street, a short walk from the Gondo Arcade and about ten minutes on foot from Zenkoji Temple. It is operated by the team behind centotto gelato, an artisan gelateria establishe

roles: boutique guesthouse (one group per night, upper floors)
chef_bio.background: CHAYADO 百八 opened on December 9, 2025 in the Higashigoshomachi district along Nagano's central shopping street, a short walk from the Gondo Arcade and about ten minutes on foot from Zenkoji Temple. It is operated by the team behind centotto gelato, an artisan gelateria established in 2022 in Shinanomachi at the foot of Mt. Kurohime, known for turning hyper-local vegetables and seasonal Nagano fruits into inventive frozen flavors.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept bridges temple-town tradition and contemporary craft: the name MOMOYA is the owner's surname rendered in kanji numerals (百八 = 108 = mo-mo-ya), and the three-story wooden lattice building is designed to offer two distinct rhythms — quiet morning reverence before Zenkoji pilgrims and lively evening energy from the arcade nearby. Centotto gelato's broader philosophy centres on food-waste reduction by sourcing off-spec seasonal produce from local farmers.
chef_bio.specialty: Matcha-forward drinks paired with centotto gelato scoops — the signature Affogato Gelato (¥750) is served in a traditional soba cup, and the layered Matcha MOMOYA Latte (¥900) showcases locally grown fruit from around Nagano Prefecture.

anecdotes.text: The building's striking wooden lattice exterior was described by local media as a 'wooden fortress' appearing on the Zenkoji approach — a deliberate design statement on a street that mixes traditional storefronts with modern retail.

Gelato is sourced from centotto gelato's Shinanomachi farm-adjacent production site, where flavors like corn, natsuhaze berry, and rhubarb are made from produce purchased directly from local growers — including imperfect 'off-spec' harvests that would otherwise go to waste.
The Affogato Gelato is served in a soba猪口 (猪口 = small cup traditionally used for soba dipping sauce or sake), a small detail that ties the modern gelato format to local craft ceramics culture.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about cross-contamination controls is published. Wheat-containing items (croquet monsieur) are prepared in the same kitchen as gelato and drinks.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce appears in the café menu (matcha drinks, gelato, corn soup). Wheat risk from this vector is low, but no allergen sheet has been published to confirm.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy (Kurohime Highland milk) is a core ingredient across gelato and latte menus. No separation or labeling for dairy-free items is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information about staff allergy training or allergy-handling procedures has been published. The café does not accept reservations (walk-in only), so pre-visit phone enquiry (026-217-2608) is the only available channel to ask about allergen handling before arriving.

positives.text: The menu is matcha and gelato-focused with minimal processed sauces — simpler ingredient profiles than a full-service restaurant, which reduces (but does not eliminate) hidden allergen risk.
centotto gelato's parent brand emphasises single-origin local produce and minimal-additive gelato-making, which may reduce hidden ingredient complexity.

cultural_comfort.note: Official site is available in English and targets international visitors to Zenkoji; tourist-area location makes English communication likely.

places.name: お寺カフェ (Otera Cafe)
places.gf_detail: Shojin cuisine often inherently avoids gluten-containing ingredients, but the use of soy sauce (contains wheat) and fu (wheat gluten) is unclear. No stated gluten-free accommodation. Celiacs should always inquire before visiting (026-234-0001). NOT confirmed celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: The signature dish, the Tera-gayu Gozen set (¥1,500), is shojin-based and centered on plant ingredients. However, because the specifications of the dashi, soy sauce, and mirin are not disclosed, the presence of hidden animal-derived components (bonito dashi, mirin, etc.) needs to be confirmed. Seasonal items such as wagashi and oshiruko are also served. Not confirmed fully vegan.
places.hours_raw: 7:30-15:30 (last order 15:00), irregular days off (Daikanjin publishes its business calendar)
places.cuisine: Cafe / shojin okayu (Buddhist rice porridge) / wagashi
places.notes: A temple cafe set inside the Shiun-kaku hall of Daikanjin, one of Zenkoji's two head temples. Since December 2024 it has been run by 'Izutsuya Tetsuji,' a sweets brand devoted to Obuse chestnuts and Shinshu-grown fruit, marking its first venture in a cafe format.

roles: カフェ運営：井筒屋鐵治（Izutsuya Tetsuji）
寺院：善光寺大勧進（天台宗本坊）
chef_bio.origin: 長野県長野市元善町（善光寺大勧進境内）
chef_bio.background: 善光寺の本坊・大勧進の紫雲閣内に設けられた寺院カフェ。 2024年12月より、小布施栗と信州産フルーツにこだわるスイーツブランド「井筒屋鐵治（いづつやてつじ）」が運営を担い、初のカフェ形態として展開している。
chef_bio.philosophy: 善光寺参拝後に身体を温め、心を整えるための食を提供。 お朝事の終了時刻に合わせて開店し、参拝のしめくくりに精進の味わいを届けることを目的としている。
chef_bio.specialty: 寺粥御膳（¥1,500）—日替わりの精進粥に根菜の煮物・豆腐料理などが添えられるセット。 小布施栗を使った栗つづり（モンブラン）や季節の和菓子・おしるこも人気。

anecdotes.text: 箸袋には大勧進の僧侶が一枚一枚手書きした漢字の言葉が記されており、会計後にお客自身が気に入ったものを選んで持ち帰ることができる。箸は無垢材製でお守り的な存在感があると評判。
紫雲閣の座敷から善光寺の山門・本堂と蓮池を一望できる。2024年11月のラジオ取材では「身も心もすっきり×ほかほか」と紹介され、朝の参拝後の穴場スポットとして注目された。

gf_cross_contamination.text: グルテンフリー対応や交差汚染防止策の記載なし。精進料理ベースではあるが、厨房での小麦使用状況は不明。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 醤油（小麦含有）の使用が料理に含まれる可能性があるが、使用する醤油の種類（小麦あり・なし）について公式の言及なし。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 隠れ動物性食材（かつおだし・みりん等）の有無について非公開。精進料理の伝統的手法に従っていればヴィーガン対応の可能性が高いが、要確認。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応方針の明示なし。問い合わせ先：026-234-0001（大勧進）またはcontact@tsumugi-wb.co.jp

positives.text: 精進料理をベースとしたメニューは動物性食材を最小限に抑える伝統に沿っており、植物性食材中心の食事が期待できる。
寺粥御膳は根菜の煮物や豆腐など植物性食材を中心とした構成と複数のレビューが報告している。

cultural_comfort.note: お寺の中にある静かな空間で、英語メニューや英語スタッフの記載なし。善光寺参拝客が主な客層。

places.name: モンゼンテラス エンヤ (Monzen Terrace Enya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or labelling. The kitchen is a full omnivore operation using standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing), wheat-based tempura batter, ramen noodles, and pizza. Shared fryer confirmed. Celiac or wheat-allergic diners should call ahead; some naturally GF items (grilled meats and vegetables, plain sashimi) may be orderable, but cross-contamination risk is real and not managed by a dedicated protocol.
places.vegan_detail: Some plant-forward items exist — vegetable tempura, avocado sushi rolls, grilled vegetables, Nozawana tempura — but the kitchen is omnivore. Ramen broth is meat-based, and all frying is done in shared oil (not segregated). Staff are noted to be accommodating with modifications (e.g., omitting mayo or meat toppings), but there is no dedicated vegan menu or allergen-separated prep.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–22:00 (last order: food 21:00, drinks 21:00); lunch 11:30–15:00, café 15:00–17:00, dinner 17:00–22:00; irregular days off
places.neighborhood: 善光寺門前 大門町 (Patio Daimon complex)
places.cuisine: Japanese cuisine / creative Japanese dishes / dining bar
places.notes: Opened in 2018 inside the Patio Daimon complex — a cluster of traditional townhouse buildings with gardens on the old gate approach to Zenkoji Temple — Monzen Terrace Enya was conceived to bring evening vitality back to a historic district that had gone quiet after dusk. The name

chef_bio.background: Opened in 2018 inside the Patio Daimon complex — a cluster of traditional townhouse buildings with gardens on the old gate approach to Zenkoji Temple — Monzen Terrace Enya was conceived to bring evening vitality back to a historic district that had gone quiet after dusk. The name plays on the Japanese word for "edge" or "veranda" (縁) and the idea of a welcoming threshold lit against the night.
chef_bio.philosophy: Three-generation family dining in a relaxed, unpretentious setting — the kitchen bridges washoku tradition with Western-leaning dishes so every member of a mixed group can find something they enjoy.
chef_bio.specialty: Sushi rolls (including a signature soba-ramen-and-roll combo at lunch) alongside grilled Shinshu beef and Fukumidori chicken using locally sourced Nagano prefecture ingredients; handmade desserts including a soba-tea choux cream and Nagano apple pie.

anecdotes.text: The terrace runs year-round: in summer it hosts an open parasol beer garden, and in winter heated bubble domes turn the outdoor seats into an atmospheric winter dining spot — unusual for inland Nagano.
A takeout café counter opens at 9:30 AM serving the restaurant's signature SO-BAR — a soba-flavored choux cream with soft serve — before the dining room opens.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shared fryer confirmed by a HappyCow reviewer: "everything is fried in the same oil." Wheat-based tempura batter is standard; no dedicated GF fryer.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned on any menu or review source. Standard wheat-containing Japanese shoyu is assumed throughout sauces, marinades, and dipping condiments.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Shared fryer used for both meat and vegetable tempura. Ramen broth is meat-based. Plant-based ordering is possible with staff guidance but kitchen is not vegan-segregated.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are praised as friendly, English-fluent, and willing to customize (e.g., omitting mayo or pork toppings on request), but no formal allergen protocol is documented.

positives.text: English menus and English-speaking staff lower the communication barrier significantly for guests with dietary needs.

cultural_comfort.note: English menus are available and staff are noted by multiple reviewers to speak English well and assist foreign visitors with dietary questions.

places.name: 戸隠つきや 門前店 (Togakure Tsukiya Monzen)
places.gf_detail: Togakushi soba noodles are made from buckwheat, which is naturally gluten-free, but standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is used in the tsuyu dipping broth. Tempura on the menu uses wheat flour batter. No dedicated GF protocol, separate fryer, or staff allergy training has been documented. Celiacs should inquire directly and treat with caution.
places.vegan_detail: The tsuyu broth is almost certainly fish-based (katsuobushi dashi), as is standard for Japanese soba. Seasonal vegetable side dishes and a vegetable croquette appear on the menu, but hidden fish dashi in sauces and broths is very likely. No vegan-specific menu or notation has been found. Ask staff specifically about dashi; navigating this without Japanese will be difficult.
places.hours_raw: Wed–Sun 11:30–14:30, 17:00–20:30 (last order 20:00); closed Mon & Tue
places.neighborhood: Zenkojishita / 善光寺門前 (Sakuraedaland-cho, near Zenkō-ji south
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner originally ran a traditional izakaya in Nagano before relocating to Tokyo's Nishi-Azabu district, where he opened the flagship SOBA & DINER 戸隠つきや. The Monzen branch, which opened in August 2024, is a homecoming of sorts — a return to Nagano and a location just steps fro

chef_bio.chef_name: Naoki Tachikawa (store manager, 立川直樹)
chef_bio.origin: Nagano City (Nagano origin), with the flagship in Nishi-Azabu, Tokyo
chef_bio.background: The owner originally ran a traditional izakaya in Nagano before relocating to Tokyo's Nishi-Azabu district, where he opened the flagship SOBA & DINER 戸隠つきや. The Monzen branch, which opened in August 2024, is a homecoming of sorts — a return to Nagano and a location just steps from Zenkō-ji temple's southern approach. The concept blends the izakaya spirit with the soba-specialist tradition.
chef_bio.philosophy: Soba as sustenance and heritage: Togakushi buckwheat has been carried by mountain ascetic practitioners (山岳修行者) as their sole permitted food during training, making it part of a living spiritual tradition. The restaurant serves it alongside foraged seasonal mountain vegetables and local Shinshu sake.
chef_bio.specialty: Togakure kirishita soba — hand-cut buckwheat noodles from fog-grown (霧下そば) highland fields in Togakushi, served in the traditional ぼっち盛り (bocchi-mori) presentation: five neat bundles arranged on a bamboo mat

anecdotes.text: The short, finely-cut noodles have a mochi-like, chewy density that surprised one reviewer — eating them felt like consuming a mass of soba rather than individual strands, a departure from conventional soba texture that divided opinion but left a strong impression.
The menu changes with the seasons: spring brings foraged wild mountain vegetables (sansai), autumn brings fresh mushrooms harvested personally by the chef from surrounding mountains.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information found about separate prep surfaces or fryers for gluten-free orders. Tempura (wheat batter) is cooked in the kitchen alongside soba, raising cross-contamination concern.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tsuyu dipping broth for soba almost certainly contains standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu), which is brewed with wheat. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative has been documented at this restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi (katsuobushi) is the standard base for soba tsuyu in Japan and is very likely present in the broth here. No vegan dashi alternative is mentioned in any source.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling protocol, allergen menu, or staff training information has been documented in any available source. Contact the restaurant directly at 026-233-8028 before visiting if dietary restrictions are serious.

positives.text: Buckwheat (soba) itself is naturally gluten-free, and plain cold zaru soba without dipping broth could be lower risk — though cross-contamination from shared kitchen equipment remains unconfirmed.

cultural_comfort.note: Casual, welcoming atmosphere with counter seating and a relaxed diner vibe; staff are friendly but the menu and ordering process are in Japanese, so basic Japanese phrases help.

places.name: 喜多平 (Kitahei)
places.gf_detail: Reviewers describe the noodles as 二八蕎麦 (niwari soba) — an 80% buckwheat / 20% wheat flour blend, which contains gluten. No dedicated gluten-free soba or 十割 (100% buckwheat) soba is mentioned anywhere. Soba tsuyu (sauce) typically contains wheat-based soy sauce. Cross-contamination risk in a shared kitchen is high. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu. Standard dashi is almost certainly katsuobushi (bonito flake) based — very common in traditional Shinshu soba shops. Zaru soba dipping sauce (tsuyu) will contain dashi from fish stock. No plant-based alternatives mentioned anywhere. Vegans should ask 「だしは何を使っていますか？」before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Wed & Fri–Sun 10:00–15:00, closed Thu
places.neighborhood: Zenkōji Nakamise-dōri (in front of the Niōmon gate)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kitahei has operated since 1861 (Bunkyu 1st year), making it one of the oldest surviving soba establishments in Nagano City. It stands immediately to the left of the Nioh Gate on Zenkoji's Nakamise-dori approach, a position it has held through Japan's Meiji Restoration and beyond

chef_bio.origin: 長野市元善町、善光寺門前
chef_bio.background: Kitahei has operated since 1861 (Bunkyu 1st year), making it one of the oldest surviving soba establishments in Nagano City. It stands immediately to the left of the Nioh Gate on Zenkoji's Nakamise-dori approach, a position it has held through Japan's Meiji Restoration and beyond. The interior preserves classic shokudo (food hall) character — a period detail: the restroom is signed '洗面所' (washing area) in the old style, not the modern term.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unpretentious, craftsman-first approach — sturdy, rustic noodles over modern presentation. The shop keeps short hours and closes Thursdays, prioritizing quality over volume.
chef_bio.specialty: Nameko soba (なめこそば) and zaru soba; also offers herring soba (ニシンそば) and tempura soba. Soba湯 (warm buckwheat water) is served after the meal. The shop uses seven-spice condiment from Yawataya Isogoro, a renowned local Nagano spice maker.

anecdotes.text: The shinise.tv heritage dining blog (which documents Japan's century-old establishments) notes that 喜多平 is 'one of the oldest soba restaurants in Nagano City,' founded in Bunkyu 1 (1861) — a period when Zenkoji drew millions of pilgrims and the temple approach was already a bustling dining corridor.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are described by reviewers as niwari soba (二八蕎麦) — 80% buckwheat, 20% wheat. No 100% buckwheat (十割) option confirmed. A shared kitchen making wheat-blend noodles and tempura presents high cross-contamination risk for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tsuyu (soba dipping sauce) at traditional Shinshu soba shops is made with wheat-containing soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu). No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternatives mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi base for tsuyu is almost certainly katsuobushi (dried bonito). No plant-based broth option found. Tempura is offered on the menu, implying shared oil with animal-derived items possible.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergy statements, or staff allergy training is mentioned in any source. The food ticket vending machine system means limited opportunity for customization at the counter.

positives.text: Buckwheat (蕎麦粉) itself is gluten-free; if a 100% buckwheat batch were available, the base grain would be safe — but no such option is confirmed here.

cultural_comfort.note: Ticket vending machine ordering, no English menu reported, and staff communication is expected in Japanese; manageable by pointing at the machine.

places.name: 太田屋 助右衛門 (Otaya Sukeemon)
places.gf_detail: The signature 十善そば (Juzen soba) is 100% buckwheat with no wheat flour added — buckwheat itself is GF. However: (1) the tsuyu dipping sauce uses standard soy sauce, which in Japan almost always contains wheat; (2) the restaurant also serves zaru soba at 80% buckwheat (20% wheat), meaning wheat noodles are produced in the same kitchen; (3) tempura batter (wheat) is prepared in the same space; (4) no dedicated GF fryer or allergen protocol is documented anywhere. Celiac diners face meaningful cross-contamination risk and the restaurant has made no GF declarations. Calling ahead is essential.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu exists. The tsuyu (soy-based dipping sauce) is almost certainly made with katsuobushi (bonito) dashi — standard for Shinshu soba shops. Accompaniments include egg roll slices and inari sushi with rice; tempura contains shrimp. Guests needing strictly plant-based should call ahead; a plain soba with no tsuyu is theoretically possible but not documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–14:00, 17:30–20:30 (closed Thu). Weekends close once sold out.
places.neighborhood: 長野市鶴賀 (Gondo Station area, near Zenkō-ji north approach)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded over 80 years ago in the back streets of Nagano city center, Otaya Sukeemon has been making handmade soba in a quiet residential neighborhood a short walk from the Gondo shopping arcade, away from the main tourist flow to Zenkō-ji. About 25 years ago the owners installed

chef_bio.background: Founded over 80 years ago in the back streets of Nagano city center, Otaya Sukeemon has been making handmade soba in a quiet residential neighborhood a short walk from the Gondo shopping arcade, away from the main tourist flow to Zenkō-ji. About 25 years ago the owners installed a 300 kg stone mill on the premises, becoming self-sufficient in buckwheat flour and cementing a philosophy of san-tate: freshly ground, freshly made, freshly boiled.
chef_bio.philosophy: San-tate (三たて) — grind only what you need, make it to order, boil it immediately. Quality buckwheat sourced from Shinshu (including Kijimadaira-grown Shinano Ichigo variety) is ground in the shop's own mill, ensuring the flour is never stale.
chef_bio.specialty: 十善そば (Juzen soba) — 100% buckwheat noodles made entirely from the shop's own stone-milled flour, a dish reportedly the first of its kind developed in Nagano. The name 十善 evokes the ten virtuous precepts associated with Zenkō-ji's Buddhist tradition.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant previously operated on Nagano's Chuo-dori main street before settling in its current house in the Tamachi/Tsuruga area, and was at some point featured on the Japanese TV programme Kenmin SHOW.
The shop is a member of the 信州そば切りの会 (Shinshu Soba Cutting Association), a guild of traditional soba artisans in Nagano Prefecture.
A 300 kg stone mill sits visibly in the dining area — guests can see it while eating, a deliberate statement about the mill-to-table philosophy.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Both 80% wheat-containing zaru soba and 100% buckwheat juzen soba are prepared in the same kitchen; no separation protocol has been documented.
Tempura (wheat batter) is a menu item; fryer separation from buckwheat items is not confirmed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsuyu dipping sauce is described as soy-based; no mention of tamari or wheat-free soy sauce anywhere in available sources. Standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Tsuyu almost certainly contains katsuobushi (bonito) dashi — no kombu-only or vegan dashi option is documented.
Tempura set includes shrimp; egg roll slices accompany lunch sets — fish and egg present in the kitchen.

positives.text: The 十善そば noodles themselves are made from 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat added — the purest buckwheat noodle on the menu.
Stone-mill self-grinding means full traceability of the buckwheat flour used in the noodles.

cultural_comfort.note: Small neighborhood soba house that draws local regulars; no English menu or signage reported — basic Japanese or pointing at dishes works fine.

places.name: 大福屋 (Daifukuya)
places.gf_detail: Tonkatsu (成満堂 lunch) is panko-breaded pork — contains wheat. Milk udon contains wheat noodles. Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) in use. No gluten-free alternatives or dedicated fryer. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The cafe's signature ぎゅうにゅうどん (milk udon, ¥450) uses dairy. Lunch is tonkatsu (breaded pork) cooked in shared fry oil. Morning sets include egg dishes and miso soup likely containing dashi. No vegan options identified.
places.hours_raw: Morning 7:30–9:30 (last order) / lunch (Jōmandō) 11:00–14:00 (last order) / café (Fri & Sat only) 14:30–19:00 (last order). Closed Wed & Thu.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 大福屋 occupies the 2nd floor of a 100-year-old former dormitory near Zenkō-ji, with a used bookstore on the ground floor. The cafe opened in December 2016. The same building hosts 成満堂 (Seimanndou), a reservation-only tonkatsu lunch counter run by Keiji Takano, a former railway comp

chef_bio.chef_name: 高野恵二 (Takano Keiji) — 成満堂; 望月仁美 (Mochizuki Hitomi) — 大福屋 cafe
chef_bio.background: 大福屋 occupies the 2nd floor of a 100-year-old former dormitory near Zenkō-ji, with a used bookstore on the ground floor. The cafe opened in December 2016. The same building hosts 成満堂 (Seimanndou), a reservation-only tonkatsu lunch counter run by Keiji Takano, a former railway company employee who retired at 50 after years of Tokyo–Nagano commuting. After retirement he completed the 88-temple Shikoku pilgrimage and trained under a veteran tonkatsu chef before opening 成満堂 roughly in 2016.
chef_bio.philosophy: Takano has said that 'the temperature at which meat tastes best is around 67°C' — his whole method is built around patience and precise heat, rejecting the shortcuts of commercial kitchens. The cafe side embraces a nostalgic Showa-era slowness: used books, cheap sweets, and hot milk in cute mugs.
chef_bio.specialty: ぎゅうにゅうどん (milk udon, ¥450) — the cafe's signature breakfast/morning item. At lunch, 成満堂 serves 信州産豚肉使用ロースかつ定食 (Shinshu pork loin cutlet set, ¥1,500), prepared by an initial high-temperature sear followed by extended low-temperature resting at 65–70°C for around 15 minutes to achieve optimal tenderness.

anecdotes.text: Takano initially planned to do volunteer decontamination work in Fukushima after quitting the railway. A retiring colleague took him to a renowned tonkatsu restaurant; the owner there taught him the basics on the spot and encouraged him to start his own. He then walked the 88-temple Shikoku pilgrimage before opening 成満堂.
By 2019 customer traffic had doubled year-over-year, with the clientele including students, elderly regulars, and solo female diners — an uncommon demographic mix for a tonkatsu specialist.

The building identifier on the cafe's Twitter handle (@dai298_monzen) references the address 222 (dai = 大 = big/no.1 prefix, 298 = ni-kyu-hachi) and 門前 (monzen = in front of the temple gate), nodding to its location in Zenkō-ji's monzenmachi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tonkatsu is panko-breaded and deep-fried; the fryer is shared and there is no information suggesting a dedicated gluten-free fryer or separate cooking area. Wheat contamination of all fried items should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned. Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is the default at this type of establishment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All morning sets include egg, dairy (Obuse milk), and likely katsuo/iriko dashi in miso soup. The signature milk udon uses dairy. No plant-based alternatives noted.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling procedures or allergen menu documentation found in any source reviewed. With only 13 seats and full reservation-only operation, direct inquiry by phone before visiting is strongly advisable.

positives.text: Fully reservation-only format (since May 2021) means small, controlled service — the chef prepares each piece to order and monitors cooking precisely, which allows for meaningful pre-visit allergy conversations.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, intimate Showa-era retro space with tatami-adjacent aesthetics; shoes-off to reach the 2F cafe, no English signage or menu noted, but the small and low-key atmosphere is welcoming to solo and female diners.

places.name: 山城屋 (Yamashiroya)
places.gf_detail: Standard Japanese soba noodles typically blend wheat flour with buckwheat (not juwari/100% buckwheat); no juwari or gluten-free noodle claim found for this restaurant. Tempura is on the menu (wheat batter). Dipping tsuyu uses wheat-based soy sauce. No dedicated GF handling, separate fryer, or allergy protocol mentioned anywhere. Celiac diners should not assume safety — inquire directly and verify noodle composition before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The menu prominently features duck soba (鴨せいろ), herring soba (にしんそば), and tempura — no vegan-specific options advertised. Dipping broth (tsuyu) at traditional soba restaurants almost universally contains bonito flake dashi (katsuo). No vegan claim or accommodation found on any source reviewed. If visiting with a group, ask whether a simple mori soba can be served with kombu-only dashi — this is not guaranteed here.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–15:00 daily; irregular days off
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A longstanding soba restaurant on the shopping street (仲見世通り) leading to Zenkoji Temple, operated by a small local company (有限会社山城屋). The shop doubles as a souvenir retail space selling oyaki (stuffed buns), regional snacks, and Shinshu food products.

chef_bio.background: A longstanding soba restaurant on the shopping street (仲見世通り) leading to Zenkoji Temple, operated by a small local company (有限会社山城屋). The shop doubles as a souvenir retail space selling oyaki (stuffed buns), regional snacks, and Shinshu food products.
chef_bio.philosophy: Emphasizes freshness and regional provenance, inviting guests to enjoy hand-made soba and dishes prepared with Shinshu (Nagano) ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-rolled soba made with Shinshu local ingredients, served in both hot and cold forms; signature sets include golden chicken nanban soba (信州黄金シャモなんばんそば) and Shinshu salmon hand-rolled sushi paired with soba.

anecdotes.text: Figure skating world champion Yuzuru Hanyu reportedly dined at Yamashiroya during a 2015 visit to Nagano.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura is served at this restaurant, indicating wheat flour frying is happening in the kitchen. No information about a dedicated fryer or separation from soba preparation was found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Japanese soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) is made with wheat-containing soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu). No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned at this restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi in traditional soba broth is typically bonito-based (katsuo dashi). Duck and herring soba are on the menu. No kombu-only or vegan dashi option is advertised.

positives.text: English menu and English-speaking staff reported available, which may help communicate dietary needs.

cultural_comfort.note: Tripadvisor notes English menu and English-speaking staff available, making it accessible to non-Japanese visitors.

places.name: 尾張屋そば店 (Owariya Soba-ten)
places.gf_detail: Soba here is nihachi (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat flour), so the noodles contain gluten. Tsuyu dipping broth is made with katsuo-bushi (bonito/wheat-containing soy sauce likely used). Tempura dishes (tenzaru, ebi tempura) use wheat batter. Shared cooking environment with no GF protocol confirmed. No dedicated GF options exist; celiac or wheat-allergic diners should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Broth confirmed made with katsuo-bushi (bonito flakes). All signature soba dishes (zaru, tororo, nameko) are served with this bonito dashi. Signature side dishes include tamago-yaki (egg roll). No vegan dashi alternative mentioned anywhere. Not vegan-friendly.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Wed, Fri, Sat, Sun 10:30–16:00, closed Thu
places.neighborhood: 元善町 (Motoyoshicho), Zenkoji approach, Nagano City
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in Taisho 7 (1918) by a proprietor originally from Owari (present-day Aichi Prefecture) — hence the name 尾張屋. The shop began as a noodle manufacturer selling udon, then pivoted to soba as Nagano's buckwheat culture took hold. Now in its third generation under Suzuki Kenta

chef_bio.background: Founded in Taisho 7 (1918) by a proprietor originally from Owari (present-day Aichi Prefecture) — hence the name 尾張屋. The shop began as a noodle manufacturer selling udon, then pivoted to soba as Nagano's buckwheat culture took hold. Now in its third generation under Suzuki Kenta, who took the reins despite having worked previously at a ryokan inn rather than in his family's kitchen, stepping up when no other successor was available.
chef_bio.philosophy: Streamlined preparation to let the soba's natural aroma and silky throat-feel (nodo-goshi) speak for itself. The third-generation owner trimmed the menu deliberately to maintain quality with a small team, while keeping the approach path to Zenkoji steeped in over a century of flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: 石臼挽き二八手打ちそば — hand-made nihachi soba (80% buckwheat, 20% wheat) ground fresh on stone mills using primarily Nagano-prefecture-grown buckwheat blended with Hokkaido varieties. The dipping broth is built on carefully selected katsuo-bushi (bonito flakes). A beloved side: the shop's signature tamago-yaki (elongated egg roll served on an ice-cream stick with grated daikon), sold for ¥100.

anecdotes.text: The founder came from Owari (Aichi Prefecture) and named the shop after his home region — an unusual origin for a pilgrimage-town soba house surrounded by locally rooted Nagano families.
The shop originally operated as a noodle manufacturer making udon, then switched to soba as buckwheat became the defining product of Nagano's culinary identity.
A blogger visiting in 2025 singled out the young staff member's hospitality — meticulous word choice and anticipatory service — as unexpectedly refined for a casual ticket-machine soba shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Nihachi soba contains 20% wheat flour; noodles are not gluten-free. No dedicated GF station or protocol has been reported at this restaurant.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dipping broth (tsuyu) is made with katsuo-bushi and almost certainly standard wheat-containing soy sauce, as is universal at traditional Japanese soba shops. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All soba broth is bonito-based. No kombu-only or vegan dashi option is indicated. Tamago-yaki (egg) is a signature side. Cross-contact with animal products throughout the kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff do not speak foreign languages; no allergy-handling protocol has been documented for this restaurant. Diners with serious allergies should call ahead (026-232-5347) with a Japanese-speaking contact.

positives.text: The restaurant holds an English menu, making basic communication slightly easier for foreign visitors, though dietary restriction conversations would require Japanese.

cultural_comfort.note: Staff do not speak foreign languages, but an English menu is available; the food ticket system at the entrance is self-explanatory, and the traditional tatami-and-table layout is straightforward for any visitor.

places.name: カフェ・テラ (Caffe Terra)
places.gf_detail: Gelato is made without artificial additives or colorants using natural ingredients. No dedicated gluten-free labeling or certification found. Pastries and cookies on the menu contain gluten. Gelato base itself is likely wheat-free but cross-contamination from shared kitchen/display cannot be ruled out without asking staff. No dedicated fryer information available (not a fried-food venue).
places.vegan_detail: Gelato is dairy-based (Shinshu milk is the signature ingredient); no vegan-specific options have been documented. Some fruit sorbets may be dairy-free but this is unconfirmed. Ask staff about specific flavors on the day.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 10:30–17:00; closed Mondays. Some sources list 11:00–17:30 on weekends — hours may vary seasonally.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] An Australian man and his Japanese wife relocated from Sydney to Nagano in 2010, drawn by the cultural richness of the Zenkō-ji pilgrimage town. They spent 2011 studying traditional gelato-making in northern Italy before opening Caffe Terra in May 2011.

chef_bio.origin: Sydney, Australia → Nagano City (2010); gelato training in northern Italy (2011)
chef_bio.background: An Australian man and his Japanese wife relocated from Sydney to Nagano in 2010, drawn by the cultural richness of the Zenkō-ji pilgrimage town. They spent 2011 studying traditional gelato-making in northern Italy before opening Caffe Terra in May 2011.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 'Terra' (Italian for earth/land) reflects a commitment to honoring local ingredients; a secondary layer of wordplay nods to the Buddhist temple just steps away (tera = temple in Japanese). Gelato is made daily in-house without artificial additives or colorants, letting the quality of the ingredient speak for itself.
chef_bio.specialty: Shinshu Rich Milk gelato — made with milk from the Kurohime Highlands — is the signature flavor, rotating alongside seasonal fruit varieties such as organic blueberries from Shinano Town, Kawanakajima white peaches, and Takayama village grapes.

anecdotes.text: Matt trained as a coffee roaster and blender, sourcing beans roasted in Melbourne to use in espresso drinks — an unusual choice that brings an Australian café culture sensibility to the Zenkō-ji temple approach.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information found on dedicated preparation surfaces or equipment for gelato. Pastries and cookies are also served in the same space, raising the possibility of shared utensils or cross-contact. Confirm with staff before ordering if celiac-level safety is required.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Gelato and espresso drinks are the core menu; soy sauce is not a component. However, pastries and cookies on the rotating menu will contain wheat. No dedicated gluten-free labeling has been documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard gelato is dairy-based (Shinshu milk is the signature ingredient). No dairy-free or vegan gelato options have been confirmed. Fruit sorbets may be dairy-free but this has not been verified in any source.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Owners are internationally experienced (Australian-Japanese) and accustomed to a wide range of customers; communication in basic English is likely possible. No formal allergy protocol has been documented — ask directly about specific flavors and ingredients on the day of your visit.

positives.text: Gelato is made in-house daily without artificial additives, colorants, or preservatives, using identifiable natural ingredients — a positive baseline for allergen transparency.

cultural_comfort.note: The owners are an Australian-Japanese couple; staff are described as very friendly and welcoming, and the café has a strong track record with international visitors to Zenkō-ji.

places.name: 楽茶れんが館 (Rakucha Renga-kan)
places.gf_detail: French bistro cuisine with no documented gluten-free accommodations. Standard menu items (hayashi rice, curry, gratin, bread) typically contain wheat flour. Sauces and roux in French/Western cooking are conventionally wheat-based. No dedicated GF options, fryer information, or allergy-handling policy found in any source. Celiac diners should call ahead in Japanese; meaningful GF accommodation is not expected here.
places.vegan_detail: Standard French bistro menu centered on meat and fish — lunch sets feature chicken or salmon; à la carte includes beef curry, hamburger steak, hayashi rice, and gratin. No vegan offerings or plant-based alternatives documented anywhere. Bread accompanies most sets (likely contains eggs/dairy). Call ahead to ask if anything can be made without animal products; do not assume.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–21:00 (lunch 11:30, last order 13:30); irregular days off
places.notes: 楽茶れんが館 occupies a registered tangible cultural property: a red brick Western-style building completed in April 1912 (Meiji 45) as the headquarters of the Shinano Chugyu Baba Transportation Company, one of the oldest Western-style brick structures in Nagano's Zenkoji temple precin

chef_bio.background: 楽茶れんが館 occupies a registered tangible cultural property: a red brick Western-style building completed in April 1912 (Meiji 45) as the headquarters of the Shinano Chugyu Baba Transportation Company, one of the oldest Western-style brick structures in Nagano's Zenkoji temple precinct. Over the decades it served as the Zenkoji Post Office (1959–1989) and then as Nagano City's local products hall (1991–2002) before being renovated into its current form as a restaurant and café. The building was formally designated a national registered tangible cultural property in 2007. It is managed by Machizukuri Nagano Co. , Ltd. , a city-development organisation.
chef_bio.philosophy: A calm, unhurried retreat in a historically significant space — the emphasis is on thoughtful French-influenced cooking inside architecture that tells the story of Nagano's modernisation era.
chef_bio.specialty: Slow-simmered special beef curry (特製ビーフカレー), served with salad and fukujin pickles — the dish that draws most repeat visitors. Dinner full-course by Chef Kitamura (minimum 6 guests, ¥12,000) is the flagship experience.

anecdotes.text: The 1912 brick building originally housed a horse-and-cattle transport company (信濃中牛馬合資会社), making it one of the few surviving Meiji-era Western brick structures in the Zenkoji approach area. Its stained glass windows are a particular draw for visitors who stumble in expecting an ordinary café.

Chef Hideo Kitamura spent 18 years as executive chef at Okushiga Kogen Hotel before taking the kitchen here; he also holds a concurrent position at Contemporary in Karuizawa, lending the dinner course a resort-level pedigree unusual for a neighbourhood bistro.
The slow-simmered beef curry — just ¥1,100 at lunch — gradually builds heat as you eat, making it a beloved everyday option for locals and pilgrims walking the Zenkoji approach.

cultural_comfort.note: Operated by a local city-development corporation; all menus and signage are in Japanese, and no English-language accommodation is documented — basic Japanese or a translation app is advisable.

places.name: 西洋料理店 もりたろう (Moritaro Western Cuisine)
places.gf_detail: Western restaurant serving pasta, pizza, and pork steak — wheat is central to most dishes. No gluten-free menu or labelling found on any source. Japanese soy sauce (含む小麦) almost certainly used in marinades. No dedicated fryer information available. Celiacs should contact the restaurant (026-237-3939) before visiting; dining safely here will be very difficult without advance coordination.
places.vegan_detail: Menu centres on Shinshu pork, pasta (likely egg-based), and pizza with dairy. No vegan menu or labelling found. Hidden animal ingredients likely throughout: miso-based sauces, butter, cream. Inquire directly, but expect very limited options.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun & holidays 11:00–14:30 (last order 14:00), 17:30–20:30 (last order 20:00); closed Mon (if Mon is a public holiday, closed the following Tue)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Moritaro sits tucked inside Patio Daimon (ぱてぃお大門), a beautifully restored cluster of Edo-period kura (storehouses) a short walk from Zenkō-ji Temple. The complex — described as a hidden alley with a small warm light glowing at its end — was renovated to house artisan shops, galle

chef_bio.background: Moritaro sits tucked inside Patio Daimon (ぱてぃお大門), a beautifully restored cluster of Edo-period kura (storehouses) a short walk from Zenkō-ji Temple. The complex — described as a hidden alley with a small warm light glowing at its end — was renovated to house artisan shops, galleries, and a handful of intimate restaurants. Moritaro occupies one of those kura spaces, its calm, dimly lit interior drawing largely a local adult clientele, especially women, who return for the carefully made, generous plates.
chef_bio.philosophy: Handmade Western cooking built on Northern Shinshu (北信濃) ingredients, touched by the seasons. The kitchen is small and output deliberate — generous portions, careful craft, no hurry.
chef_bio.specialty: 信州特選ポーク香草ステーキ (Shinshu Premium Pork Herb Steak, 250g) — thick-cut Shinshu Orein pork seared with aromatic herbs and finished with olive oil; described as exceptionally tender with a clean, light finish despite its richness.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant appeared on TBS's popular travel-food programme バナナマンのせっかくグルメ (Banana Man's Sekkaku Gourme) on 31 July 2022, bringing regional and national attention to the Shinshu pork herb steak. By 2026 the dining room was filling to capacity by 11:30 AM on weekdays, with a waitlist forming before noon.
A 2026 visitor noted that despite the tiny 7-seat room, dishes arrived within about ten minutes of ordering via QR code — a pace that keeps the intimate space feeling unhurried rather than cramped.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Pasta and pizza are core menu items — wheat flour is used throughout the kitchen. Cross-contamination risk for celiacs is high and no source mentions any separation protocols.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Marinades for the pork steak and miso-based dishes likely use standard Japanese soy sauce containing wheat. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned on any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Butter, cream, and meat stock are expected throughout; the kitchen is not set up for plant-based cooking and no vegan accommodation is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergen chart, or documented allergy-handling procedure found across Tabelog, official site, or any blog. Phone enquiry (026-237-3939) before visiting is strongly recommended for any dietary restriction.

positives.text: Credit cards accepted (VISA, Master, JCB); wheelchair accessible with no steps; non-smoking; children and strollers welcome — broadly accessible venue even if dietary accommodation is unconfirmed.

cultural_comfort.note: No English signage or menu confirmed; ordering is via QR code in Japanese — a few words of Japanese or pointing will get you through.

places.name: MACCHA HOUSE 抹茶館 善光寺仲見世通り店 (Maccha House Zenkoji Nakamise-dori)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options or labeling anywhere on the menu or official site. Signature items contain wheat: tiramisu uses mascarpone cream layered on sponge/ladyfinger (wheat), and no dedicated fryer is indicated. Cross-contamination risk is high in a small café kitchen. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around dairy-heavy desserts: tiramisu uses mascarpone, soft-serve ice cream is dairy, matcha lattes use milk, and red bean sweets may contain egg. No plant-milk substitutes or vegan claims are mentioned on the official site or any review source consulted.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–19:00 (last order 30 min before closing), irregular days off
places.notes: MACCHA HOUSE is a matcha specialty chain that opened its first Japan location in Kyoto Kawaramachi in May 2015. The Zenkoji Nakamise-dori branch, the brand's first Nagano Prefecture outpost, opened on May 1, 2023, taking over the space formerly occupied by soba restaurant Momotos

chef_bio.background: MACCHA HOUSE is a matcha specialty chain that opened its first Japan location in Kyoto Kawaramachi in May 2015. The Zenkoji Nakamise-dori branch, the brand's first Nagano Prefecture outpost, opened on May 1, 2023, taking over the space formerly occupied by soba restaurant Momotose. The brand has since grown to roughly a dozen locations across Japan plus one in Singapore.
chef_bio.philosophy: Using only carefully selected, highly fragrant matcha chosen by skilled Kyoto tea masters from Morihan, to create sweets and beverages that showcase the depth of Japanese tea culture for a wide audience.
chef_bio.specialty: 宇治抹茶のティラミス — a silky mascarpone tiramisu dusted with premium matcha powder, sourced from Morihan, a Kyoto tea house operating since the early 19th century. Also notable: concentrated matcha soft-serve and seasonal matcha shaved ice.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies the former site of the beloved local soba restaurant Momotose, and the transition was noted with some nostalgia by Nagano residents even as they welcomed the new matcha destination.

Second-floor counter seats that face the window give diners a direct view toward Zenkoji Temple while they sip their matcha lattes — a detail that has made the spot popular for solo visitors on temple pilgrimage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about cross-contamination controls is published. The kitchen produces tiramisu (wheat-containing sponge/ladyfinger), soft serve, and shaved ice in a compact two-floor café setup — shared equipment is very likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce is used in a matcha dessert menu, but wheat appears structurally in tiramisu and parfait components (sponge cake / ladyfingers). No allergen sheet is published online.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy is pervasive across the menu (mascarpone, soft-serve ice cream, milk for lattes). No non-dairy alternatives are offered, so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official store page states only 'for details, please inquire with staff' — no written allergen matrix or dedicated allergy protocol is described.

cultural_comfort.note: A tourist-facing chain café on the main Zenkoji approach street; signage and ordering are straightforward even without Japanese, and the venue regularly serves international visitors.

places.name: ポルカドットカフェ (Polka Dot Cafe)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or protocol documented anywhere. Regular soy sauce (wheat-containing) is almost certainly used in the washoku-style set meals and marinades. Tempura (wheat batter) appears as a side dish. One source noted pumpkin karaage uses potato starch and rice flour, which is an incidental GF-friendly coating, but this is not offered as a GF dish. The kitchen has not published any cross-contamination controls. Celiac diners should not eat here without direct confirmation from the kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: The daily rotating set meal (一汁八菜) is built around washoku fundamentals — miso soup, dashi-simmered vegetables, and egg dishes — making hidden bonito dashi (かつおだし) very likely. One blog noted a shirasu (whitebait) egg omelette among side dishes. No vegan-designated menu exists. Plant-based diners should ask in advance; partial accommodation may be possible given the wide spread of vegetable small dishes, but the kitchen has made no documented vegan commitment.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 11:30–14:30 (last order 14:00), 18:00–21:00 (last order 21:00); weekends & holidays 11:30–16:00, 18:00–21:00; closed Wed & Thu
places.neighborhood: Gondō-chō / within walking distance of Gondō Stn
places.cuisine: Traditional-house café, Japanese set meals, shaved ice (kakigōri)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yamada-san trained at a formal Japanese restaurant in Shinjuku, Tokyo, honing professional washoku technique before returning to Nagano to open his own place.

chef_bio.origin: Opened March 20, 2018 in a renovated old machiya townhouse in the Gondo shopping district near Zenkoji.
chef_bio.background: Yamada-san trained at a formal Japanese restaurant in Shinjuku, Tokyo, honing professional washoku technique before returning to Nagano to open his own place.
chef_bio.philosophy: Yamada-san's core conviction is that winter is actually the best season for kakigori — he ate extensively through Tokyo's shaved-ice scene, became convinced that the scarcity of year-round kakigori in Nagano was a gap worth filling, and built the cafe around that idea alongside serious washoku lunches.
chef_bio.specialty: Year-round kakigori (shaved ice) and a daily-changing one-soup, eight-side-dish Japanese set meal built around Nagano Prefecture seasonal vegetables.

anecdotes.text: Ice is temperature-managed to approximately -8°C on the surface before shaving, producing a notably soft, airy texture rather than the coarse crunch of typical machine ice.
Lunch reservations for the 11:30 seating fill quickly; the daily menu is announced each morning only on Instagram Stories, making spontaneous visits a small adventure.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No cross-contamination controls have been documented. The kitchen produces tempura (wheat batter) and other wheat-containing dishes alongside everything else, with no published separation procedures.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy sauce is used throughout the washoku set meals, marinades (e.g., ginger pork shogayaki), and dipping preparations. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce has been mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi-simmered vegetable dishes and miso soup are core to every set meal; bonito dashi (katsuo) is standard in washoku cooking and is not flagged as absent. Egg dishes (tamagoyaki, shirasu omelette) are regularly listed among the rotating side dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: One reviewer noted that staff accommodated a request to reduce rice and miso soup portions and offered refills on request, suggesting general flexibility, but no allergy-specific protocol has been documented.

positives.text: The pumpkin karaage side dish uses potato starch and rice flour as its coating — incidentally gluten-free frying, though not offered as a dedicated GF item.
The set meal format naturally includes many vegetable-based small dishes, giving partial accommodation more room than a single-dish restaurant would.

cultural_comfort.note: No English-language presence; menus, Instagram, and all signage are Japanese-only, but the casual counter atmosphere is welcoming to anyone who can point and order.

places.name: とんかつ専門店 からり (Tonkatsu Semmonten Karari)
places.gf_detail: Tonkatsu is breaded with wheat flour and panko (wheat breadcrumbs) — wheat is the core of the product, not an incidental ingredient. Miso soup typically contains wheat-based shoyu. Fried shrimp shares oil. No source mentions any GF accommodation, alternative breading, or allergy protocol. Celiac or severe wheat-allergic diners cannot safely eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Entirely centered on pork — tonkatsu, pork loin sets, and fried shrimp. No plant-based mains exist. Miso soup is served as standard. Nothing on the menu or any source suggests a vegan option.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Wed, Fri–Sun & holidays 11:30–14:30 / 17:30–20:00, closed Thu
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Karari opened on 8 August 2019 in a ground-floor space of the Daimoncho Forest building, a short walk from Zenkoji temple's main gate. The name 'からり' is a Japanese onomatopoeia for a light, crisp sound — evoking the ideal tonkatsu fry.

chef_bio.origin: 長野市大門町、善光寺門前
chef_bio.background: Karari opened on 8 August 2019 in a ground-floor space of the Daimoncho Forest building, a short walk from Zenkoji temple's main gate. The name 'からり' is a Japanese onomatopoeia for a light, crisp sound — evoking the ideal tonkatsu fry.
chef_bio.philosophy: Quality ingredient selection over elaboration: premium domestic pork sourced from the Wapork Mochipork brand, rice from northern Nagano described as a rare local variety, and a simple preparation philosophy that lets the pork speak for itself.
chef_bio.specialty: Thick-cut loin cutlet made with 'Wapork Mochipork' (和豚もち豚), a branded domestic pork prized for its sweet-flavored fat and mochi-like texture. The restaurant's motto is '塩だけで旨い' — delicious with just salt — reflecting confidence that the meat needs no sauce to shine.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers frequently note that staff proactively offer unlimited refills of rice, miso soup, and cabbage — a generous touch at a restaurant in the ¥1,000–¥2,000 lunch range.
One reviewer described the mochipork loin as 'soft enough to eat without teeth,' capturing the unusually tender texture that the branded pork is known for.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tonkatsu is coated in wheat flour and panko; wheat is intrinsic to the dish, not a cross-contamination risk — it is the primary allergen present. No dedicated fryer for GF items exists or is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No allergen breakdown is published. Standard Japanese miso soup and sauces served alongside tonkatsu routinely contain wheat-based shoyu; no GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork is fried in the same oil used for all items including shrimp. No plant-based items are on the menu, so vegan cross-contact is moot — the menu is entirely animal-based.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No source mentions any allergy inquiry protocol or staff training in allergen handling. The restaurant does not appear to have published any allergen chart or accommodation policy.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, serious tonkatsu specialist near Zenkoji with no English signage or menu noted; staff are described as warm and attentive, but communication will be in Japanese.

places.name: 信州 おいも工房 (Shinshu Oimo Kobo)
places.gf_detail: Sweet potatoes themselves are GF, and simple preparations (roasted sweet potato, plain daigaku-imo, plain kenpi) are likely GF. However, Shinshu miso seasoning on kenpi may contain barley; gelato/shake base ingredients are not confirmed GF. No dedicated GF labeling, no allergen chart available online. Cross-contamination from shared preparation surfaces unknown. Celiacs should ask before ordering any seasoned or blended item.
places.vegan_detail: Many items are plant-based by nature — roasted sweet potato (熟成焼き芋), candied sweet potato (大学芋/蜜大学芋), and imo-kenpi chips are typically vegan. Gelato (モンブランジェラート) uses Shinshu dairy milk, so is not vegan. Sweet potato shakes likely contain milk. Savory kenpi flavors include Shinshu miso (麦麹 barley-koji miso is common — ask) and chili.
