No dedicated vegan labeling; select items carefully and confirm with staff.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays, weekends & public holidays 10:30–16:30, irregular days off
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 信州おいも工房 opened in March 2022 on Zenkoji's historic Nakamise-dori approach street in Nagano City. It is operated by おいも日和 (Oimo Biyori), a small Nagano-based company whose flagship store is in Azumino. The shop was conceived as a way to bring locally grown Shinshu sweet potatoes d

chef_bio.background: 信州おいも工房 opened in March 2022 on Zenkoji's historic Nakamise-dori approach street in Nagano City. It is operated by おいも日和 (Oimo Biyori), a small Nagano-based company whose flagship store is in Azumino. The shop was conceived as a way to bring locally grown Shinshu sweet potatoes directly to visitors of one of Japan's most famous temples.
chef_bio.philosophy: The company cultivates sweet potatoes from the soil up — starting from their own farm and partnering with contract growers across northern Nagano — and ages harvested potatoes for 60 or more days to concentrate their natural sweetness before any product is made.
chef_bio.specialty: Ultra-thin imo-kenpi (善光寺ケンピ) in distinctive Shinshu flavors such as Hachimanya Isogoro chili and Shinshu miso, plus honey-glazed sweet potato (蜜大学芋), roasted sweet potato shakes, and dual-cream monblanc gelato (蜜芋ふたいろ♪モンブランジェラート).

anecdotes.text: The owner hand-built custom machinery to slice kenpi to an extraordinary 3 mm thickness — far thinner than typical imo-kenpi — giving the snacks a distinctive crisp texture that became the shop's calling card.
Opened on March 19, 2022, the shop was positioned on Nakamise-dori specifically to bring farm-fresh Shinshu sweet potato sweets to the steady flow of pilgrims and tourists visiting Zenkoji.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No allergen chart or cross-contamination information is available online. The shop uses shared preparation equipment; dedicated fryer status is unknown. Celiacs cannot assume safety without asking staff directly.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce-based items identified on the menu. Shinshu miso seasoning used on some kenpi flavors may contain barley (麦麹), which is not GF. Confirm before ordering miso-flavored items.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Gelato and shakes are made with Shinshu dairy milk and are prepared at the same counter as plant-based items. No information on dedicated utensils for vegan preparation.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information found about English-language allergy communication or dedicated allergy protocols. The shop is a small walk-up stall; allergy inquiries in Japanese are advisable before ordering.

positives.text: Core menu items — roasted sweet potato (熟成焼き芋) and plain honey daigaku-imo — are single-ingredient preparations with no obvious gluten sources, making them among the safer choices for those avoiding wheat.
Sweet potatoes are naturally gluten-free and the shop's entire concept is built around them, meaning the majority of menu items start from a GF-compatible base ingredient.

cultural_comfort.note: Walk-up street stall with no staff interaction required beyond ordering; no English menus confirmed, but items are visible and pointing suffices.

places.name: つち茂 (Tsuchimo)
places.gf_detail: Oyaki dough is made with wheat flour (小麦粉) as a primary ingredient — this is a wheat-based product by definition. No gluten-free options are offered or mentioned. Not safe for celiac or gluten-intolerant diners.
places.vegan_detail: Several oyaki fillings are vegetable-based (nozawana pickled greens, eggplant, kabocha, mushroom medley, mixed vegetables) and may be vegan in filling, but the dough is standard wheat-flour based and soy sauce is used in seasonings — hidden animal-derived ingredients (bonito dashi in soy sauce or fillings) cannot be ruled out without staff confirmation. The signature negi-miso cheese oyaki contains dairy. No vegan labeling or certification.
places.hours_raw: 9:00–17:00 daily; no regular closing days. Occasional Wednesday closures have been noted (e.g., specific Wednesdays in June on their online shop).
places.neighborhood: Zenkoji Nakamise-dori (善光寺仲見世通り), Nagano City
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] つち茂 (Tsuchimo) is a Nagano landmark established in the Edo period (1700s), now in its 18th generation of the founding family. Originally a souvenir and bean confectionery shop on the Zenkoji Nakamise-dori pilgrimage approach, the shop added freshly baked oyaki to its lineup rough

chef_bio.background: つち茂 (Tsuchimo) is a Nagano landmark established in the Edo period (1700s), now in its 18th generation of the founding family. Originally a souvenir and bean confectionery shop on the Zenkoji Nakamise-dori pilgrimage approach, the shop added freshly baked oyaki to its lineup roughly 30 years ago during the Heisei era, which has since become its calling card.
chef_bio.philosophy: A three-part small business — souvenir shop, bean merchant, and oyaki bakery — that has served pilgrims and visitors to Zenkoji for roughly three centuries, balancing tradition with crowd-pleasing modern flavors like negi-miso cheese.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly baked oyaki — especially the signature negi-miso cheese (ねぎ味噌チーズ) variety, grilled on an iron griddle and served hot at the counter. Traditional nozawana-filled oyaki is also a staple.

anecdotes.text: The shop is currently run by the 18th-generation proprietor, making it one of the longest continuously operated family businesses on the Zenkoji approach road.
Tsuchimo's shop mascot is a dog named Kentaro (健太郎), who features on their packaging and online shop — a charming detail that regulars look out for.

The negi-miso cheese oyaki, priced around ¥230–¥250, is described by visitors as unexpectedly rich with melted cheese inside a chewy wheat dough — a modern twist that draws queues alongside the traditional nozawana variety.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oyaki dough is wheat-flour based by recipe — this is not a cross-contamination risk but a fundamental ingredient issue. The product is not suitable for anyone avoiding gluten.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is used in oyaki fillings (confirmed in general oyaki ingredient descriptions for similar Nagano shops); standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No allergen segregation information is available. Multiple fillings are prepared in the same shop including dairy-containing (cheese) and fish-based seasonings. Cross-contact between fillings cannot be excluded.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy handling protocol, or dietary labeling is listed on the shop's website or any review source. Staff interaction is minimal at this walk-up counter format. Diners with serious allergies should contact the shop directly before visiting.

positives.text: Vegetable-filled oyaki varieties (nozawana, eggplant, kabocha, mushroom) are plant-based in their primary filling ingredients, which may suit some dietary preferences — but full ingredient disclosure is not publicly available.

cultural_comfort.note: A street-side takeaway counter with no English signage noted; staff interaction is minimal (point and pay), but ordering is straightforward as oyaki are displayed and priced clearly.

places.name: トラットリア ディベルテンテ (Trattoria DiverTente)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options are advertised. The kitchen uses a blend of Shinshu wheat flour and Italian imported flour for its Neapolitan pizzas, and standard pasta is served. No dedicated GF menu or GF claims found across any source. The restaurant is a general Italian kitchen — wheat is central. Those with celiac disease should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; cross-contamination risk is high given active pizza and pasta production.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan options are listed. The menu features Italian cuisine focused on Shinshu local produce, venison, pork (Shinshu Taro Pork), and cheese-topped pizzas. Pasta dishes may use egg-based noodles and meat-based sauces. Dairy (cream cheese pizza, quiche) and meat are central to the menu. Possible to ask about vegetable-forward pasta dishes given the strong seasonal-produce focus, but nothing is advertised as vegan.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:30 (LO 14:00), Dinner 17:30–20:30 (LO 20:00); closed Wednesdays; hours may vary — confirm before visiting
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner-chef Iwata Shunsuke spent over 14 years working at hotels in Nagano Prefecture, building expertise across French, Italian, pastry and bread-making, teppanyaki, and banquet cuisine. He opened Trattoria DiverTente in June 2019 as his independent venture, choosing Italian as t

chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Iwata Shunsuke spent over 14 years working at hotels in Nagano Prefecture, building expertise across French, Italian, pastry and bread-making, teppanyaki, and banquet cuisine. He opened Trattoria DiverTente in June 2019 as his independent venture, choosing Italian as the vehicle for an accessible, casual neighborhood restaurant near Zenkō-ji Temple's western gate.
chef_bio.philosophy: Iwata's guiding principle is 'approachable × enjoyable × delicious. ' He intentionally avoids rigid recipes, shaping each dish around whatever seasonal Shinshu ingredients are at their peak — zucchini and wild vegetables in spring/summer, eggplant and sweet potatoes in autumn. The restaurant name 'Divertente' means 'fun' in Italian, and reflects his goal that dining here should feel relaxed and joyful for everyone.
chef_bio.specialty: Shinshu-sourced venison (gibier) dishes and Neapolitan-style pizza made with a proprietary blend of local Shinshu wheat and Italian imported flour

anecdotes.text: Iwata partners with a local licensed meat-processing facility to source properly handled venison, bringing the same care to his 200g+ venison hamburger (enriched with aromatic vegetables and olive oil) and venison rump steak (served with a mixed berry sauce of currants and blackberries) as he would to fine-hotel banquet cookery.

The restaurant sits one minute's walk from Zenkō-ji Temple's rear gate, in a distinctive white-and-green building with an Italian flag — a quiet alternative to the main Omotesandō shopping street that many visitors never find.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available on cross-contamination controls. Active pizza and pasta production means wheat flour is in continuous use throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Italian cuisine focus means soy sauce is unlikely in main dishes, but no explicit confirmation that wheat-based soy sauce is absent from all preparations. Confirm with staff for any dish that may use Japanese-inflected seasoning (e.g., the Shinshu miso pizza).

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy (cream cheese, quiche) and meat (venison, pork) are core menu components; no segregated vegan prep surfaces are mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or trained allergy protocol has been described in any source reviewed. Phone reservation (026-466-6650) is the recommended channel to discuss dietary needs before visiting.

positives.text: Strong seasonal, locally sourced produce ethos — the owner explicitly shapes menus around available vegetables, which may allow for ad-hoc vegetable-forward dishes when asked.

cultural_comfort.note: English service status is unknown; the restaurant is a small owner-operated trattoria where interaction is primarily in Japanese, though a warm, casual atmosphere makes it approachable.

places.name: 萬佳亭 (Mankatei)
places.gf_detail: Standard Japanese kaiseki relies heavily on wheat-based soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu) in marinades, sauces, and seasoning throughout the meal. Dashi is typically fish-based. No gluten-free menu or dedicated GF handling is advertised. Cross-contamination risk is high in a traditional kaiseki kitchen. Guests with celiac disease should call ahead, and even then safe accommodation cannot be assumed.
places.vegan_detail: Traditional kaiseki cuisine — nearly every course will use dashi made from katsuobushi (bonito flakes) or niboshi, and soy sauce containing wheat. No vegan menu is offered. Guests with strict plant-based requirements should contact the restaurant well in advance; accommodation is uncertain.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–14:00, 17:00–21:00 (closed Wed, plus occasional irregular closures)
places.notes: Founded in Meiji 38 (1905) within Shiroyama Park, a hilltop park adjacent to Zenkoji Temple, 萬佳亭 is one of Nagano city's oldest surviving ryotei. The restaurant's name is rooted in a piece of local lore: when Emperor Meiji surveyed the Zenkoji Plain during his provincial tour, he

chef_bio.origin: 長野市東之門町・城山公園（明治38年創業）
chef_bio.background: Founded in Meiji 38 (1905) within Shiroyama Park, a hilltop park adjacent to Zenkoji Temple, 萬佳亭 is one of Nagano city's oldest surviving ryotei. The restaurant's name is rooted in a piece of local lore: when Emperor Meiji surveyed the Zenkoji Plain during his provincial tour, he reportedly exclaimed 「絶景かな」(what magnificent scenery), and the establishment took its spirit from that moment of imperial wonder.
chef_bio.philosophy: Seasonal kaiseki showcasing the bounty of Shinshu (Nagano Prefecture) — mountain and sea ingredients brought together in refined multi-course presentation, served with panoramic views of the Zenkoji Plain.
chef_bio.specialty: 四季の信州食材を用いた山楼海鮮懐石

anecdotes.text: The name 萬佳亭 derives from Emperor Meiji's exclamation of wonder at the view of the Zenkoji Plain during a regional tour; the restaurant has traded on that panoramic legacy since 1905.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free handling information is published. A traditional kaiseki kitchen uses wheat-based soy sauce throughout multiple courses; cross-contamination is a structural risk rather than an exception.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese kaiseki cuisine uses koikuchi soy sauce (wheat-containing) in marinades, dipping sauces, and simmered dishes. No tamari or GF soy sauce option is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi (katsuobushi, niboshi) is the foundation of traditional kaiseki seasoning and is present in nearly every course. No plant-based dashi alternative is advertised.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu or allergy-handling protocol is published. The Nagano CVB listing notes English menus are available, but makes no mention of dietary accommodation procedures.

positives.text: English menus are available, which may ease communication of dietary needs.

cultural_comfort.note: English menus are available according to the Nagano City tourism board, and the restaurant regularly serves tourists visiting Zenkoji Temple.

places.name: 日本料理 そば懐石 紡ぎ (Nihon Ryori Soba Kaiseki Tsumugi)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant's centerpiece is house-milled 100% buckwheat (十割) soba — no binding wheat flour in the noodles themselves — but a traditional kaiseki menu almost certainly uses wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu) in sauces, dressings, and simmered dishes, and dashi may contain cross-contamination risk. No dedicated GF protocol or fryer separation has been documented. Celiacs should call ahead to discuss course modifications; safe dining cannot be assumed without direct staff confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: Traditional kaiseki format relies heavily on katsuobushi (bonito) and konbu dashi, and the signature soba courses include meat and seafood elements (wagyu, venison, mullet roe). One source notes vegetarian modifications are "partially available" with advance notice, but full vegan accommodation has not been confirmed. Diners must contact the restaurant in advance to discuss options.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 12:00–15:00 (last order 13:00) / 18:00–22:00 (last order 19:00); closed Sun & Mon (if Mon is a public holiday, open Mon and closed Tue); closed for New Year Dec 29–Jan 4
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Born 1980 in Nagano City, Machida Tetsuya trained at the Michelin two-star kaiseki restaurant 「つる幸」in Kanazawa before going on to refine his soba technique at 「そば処 木鶏 (Kidori)」in Nagano. Inspired by the region's seasonal bounty, he returned to Shinshu to open Tsumugi in March 202

chef_bio.background: Born 1980 in Nagano City, Machida Tetsuya trained at the Michelin two-star kaiseki restaurant 「つる幸」in Kanazawa before going on to refine his soba technique at 「そば処 木鶏 (Kidori)」in Nagano. Inspired by the region's seasonal bounty, he returned to Shinshu to open Tsumugi in March 2022 inside the historic Kurananiwa courtyard of the Patio Daimon complex, steps from the Zenko-ji pilgrimage approach. His wife, 町田 由起子 (Yukiko), serves as hostess and co-proprietor.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 紡ぎ (tsumugi, 'weaving') captures the restaurant's mission: threading together the bounty of Shinshu nature, local producers, traditional technique, and the people who gather at the table — whether celebrating a milestone or mourning a loss. Every course is designed to slow time down and let guests savour Nagano's terroir through all five senses.
chef_bio.specialty: 100% buckwheat soba kaiseki — house-milled daily on a stone mill using Shinshu cultivars (Norisawa, Nagawa) and Togakushi spring water, woven into a nine-to-twelve-course omakase alongside seasonal Shinshu vegetables, venison from the Bi-Gasshara Plateau, and Shinshu Premium Wagyu.

anecdotes.text: Machida trained at Michelin 2-star 「つる幸」in Kanazawa before pivoting to soba-kaiseki, a combination inspired by a family trip that led him to train further at 「そば処 木鶏」in Nagano.
Tsumugi is housed in a Taisho-era warehouse within the Patio Daimon complex, where exposed wooden beams provide an atmospheric backdrop for a ten-seat counter experience.

Signature venison from the Bi-Gasshara Plateau is slow-cooked until tender and finished with locally produced honey and balsamic vinegar — a dish that appeared in Nagano's jibier (game) promotion coverage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen protocol has been documented. Traditional kaiseki cooking uses shared equipment across courses that contain wheat-based soy sauce, which poses cross-contamination risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaiseki cuisine universally uses standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu), which contains wheat. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative has been mentioned by the restaurant or in any review.

Multi-course soba kaiseki includes simmered dishes, sauces, and dressings that typically rely on wheat-containing shoyu; the buckwheat soba noodles themselves are 100% buckwheat but share a kitchen with these preparations.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Katsuobushi (bonito flake) dashi is the standard base in traditional kaiseki; no plant-based dashi alternative has been confirmed. Courses include meat and seafood throughout.

staff_allergy_handling.text: One traveller review noted staff were 'very kind of you to work around our allergies with creativity,' suggesting willingness to accommodate, but this was informal and course-level substitutions cannot be guaranteed.
English-speaking staff are available and English menus are provided, which facilitates allergy communication, but no formal allergy protocol is publicly documented.

positives.text: The soba noodles are 100% pure buckwheat (十割), house-milled daily — no wheat flour binder used in the noodles themselves, unlike the majority of soba restaurants.
Reservation-only format with a tiny 10-seat room gives the chef the ability to discuss dietary needs personally at the time of booking.

cultural_comfort.note: English-speaking staff and an English menu are available; online reservations via TableCheck make booking straightforward for non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: 山路 (Yamaji)
places.gf_detail: Soba noodles in Japan are commonly blended with wheat flour (ni-hachi style, 20% wheat) unless explicitly stated as juwari (100% buckwheat). No source confirms this shop uses juwari soba. The kitchen also produces udon (100% wheat) and curry, meaning wheat cross-contact is a serious risk. The standard tsuyu dipping sauce contains wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free accommodation or allergy awareness has been documented. Celiac diners should not eat here without direct verbal confirmation of noodle type and sauce ingredients.
places.vegan_detail: Standard soba tsuyu at traditional Japanese soba shops is made with katsuo (bonito) dashi; no plant-based broth option has been documented here. The menu also includes curry and udon, with no indication of vegan adaptations. Assume all dipping and warm broths contain fish stock.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed, Thu, Sun & holidays: 11:00 until sold out (around 24:00 as a guide); Fri & Sat: from around 17:00; closed Tue
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small handmade-soba and udon shop sitting within a souvenir arcade on the Zenkoji temple approach (仲見世通り), Nagano City. The shop doubles as a craft and gift space, reflecting the owner's eclectic interests — visitors report cycling gear and pottery among the décor.

chef_bio.background: A small handmade-soba and udon shop sitting within a souvenir arcade on the Zenkoji temple approach (仲見世通り), Nagano City. The shop doubles as a craft and gift space, reflecting the owner's eclectic interests — visitors report cycling gear and pottery among the décor.
chef_bio.philosophy: A neighbourhood spot with a relaxed, slightly bohemian feel: jazz plays through JBL speakers and the owner is known to hold court on topics ranging from audio equipment to the history of Zenkoji Temple.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-pulled soba noodles made with Nagano Prefecture buckwheat flour, served cold (seiru) or warm.

anecdotes.text: Multiple visitors note that jazz plays through JBL speakers and the owner is happy to chat about his audio setup and Zenkoji history, giving the shop an unexpectedly intimate, parlour-like atmosphere for a temple-district soba stop.
The shop can be entered either from the Nakamise-dori side (between a washi-goods store and a rice-cracker shop) or from the quieter temple lodging lane (宿坊通り) on the opposite side.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen produces udon (pure wheat) alongside soba, making shared surfaces and water a near-certain cross-contact risk. No separation or allergen protocol has been documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soba tsuyu is made with soy sauce that contains wheat. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative has been reported for this establishment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Katsuo (bonito) dashi is the expected base for all soba broth and tsuyu here; no plant-based broth option is documented. Fish-stock contact unavoidable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling protocol, allergen menu, or English allergy communication has been documented at this restaurant. Inquire directly in Japanese before ordering.

positives.text: Soba is freshly hand-pulled on premises from local Nagano buckwheat flour — if the noodle ratio and sauce ingredients can be confirmed, there is a potential path for a gluten-sensitive (non-celiac) diner eating plain cold soba without tsuyu.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighbourhood soba counter tucked inside a souvenir arcade; the owner is reportedly warm and chatty but signage, menus, and conversation are in Japanese only.

places.name: 吉祥庵 (Kissho-an)
places.gf_detail: Despite Tabelog's 十割 (100% buckwheat) category tag, multiple independent sources confirm this restaurant serves 二八 soba — 80% buckwheat, 20% wheat flour. The noodles themselves contain wheat. Additionally, tsuyu (dipping broth) is made with conventional soy sauce (wheat-containing), and the menu includes tempura with wheat batter. No dedicated GF options or allergen handling is mentioned anywhere. Not safe for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is clearly bonito (katsuo) based — reviewers specifically highlight its strong katsuo flavor. Standard soy-sauce tsuyu also contains wheat. No vegan alternatives are listed.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 11:00–17:00 (last order 16:30), Sun 11:00–15:00, closed Mon (also closed the day after a public holiday), closes once soba sells out
places.neighborhood: 東後町・権堂 (Higashi-go-cho / Gondo), Zenkoji approach area
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] An independent neighborhood soba shop on the Zenkoji approach corridor in Nagano's Higashi-go-cho district, within walking distance of Zenkoji Temple. The restaurant distinguishes itself by grinding its own buckwheat in-house with a stone mill, with the milling area visible from

chef_bio.background: An independent neighborhood soba shop on the Zenkoji approach corridor in Nagano's Higashi-go-cho district, within walking distance of Zenkoji Temple. The restaurant distinguishes itself by grinding its own buckwheat in-house with a stone mill, with the milling area visible from the entrance. It is not affiliated with the national 吉祥庵 chain (Four Seeds Corporation).
chef_bio.philosophy: 挽き立て・打ち立て・茹で立て — freshly ground, freshly rolled, freshly boiled; the restaurant's tagline is 'soba embodies spirit; soba is appreciated for its aroma. '
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade soba set lunch — zaru soba or kake soba with a mini tempura bowl, cold tofu, soba dumplings, and pickles; known for generous portions at low prices (weekday lunch around ¥980)

anecdotes.text: The stone mill used to grind buckwheat on-site is visible from the entrance, signaling the restaurant's commitment to fresh, house-ground flour.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles contain wheat (二八 soba is 20% wheat flour); tempura is battered with wheat flour. Cross-contamination risk is high in a soba kitchen where wheat is used routinely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard tsuyu (dipping broth) is made with katsuo dashi and conventional soy sauce, which contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce or tamari alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito (katsuo) dashi is central to the broth; reviewers specifically note the strong bonito flavor. No plant-based broth option is offered.

positives.text: Buckwheat itself is 80% of the noodle composition; those with wheat allergy (not celiac-level intolerance) should confirm with staff, though no allergy protocol is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood soba shop catering primarily to local Japanese diners; no English menu or staff English noted, though the casual counter/tatami setting and simple ordering process are manageable without much Japanese.

places.name: とんかつ成満堂 (Tonkatsu Jomando)
places.gf_detail: Tonkatsu is coated in panko breadcrumbs (wheat), making the main dish unsafe for celiacs. The sides (rice, miso soup, tofu) may be gluten-free individually, but no dedicated GF fryer or allergen protocol is documented anywhere. Soy sauce used in house sauce likely contains wheat. Ask directly about side dishes if wheat-sensitive.
places.vegan_detail: Entirely pork-focused; sets include pork-based miso soup and the restaurant's identity is built around premium pig breeds. No vegan options exist.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–13:30 (Mon–Tue, Fri–Sun); Dinner 17:00–19:00 (Fri–Sat only); Closed Wed–Thu. Note: some sources list closed days as Tue–Wed — confirm by phone before visiting.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Takano spent decades working for a railway company, commuting between Tokyo and Nagano. At age 50 he took early retirement due to health concerns from years of shift work, with no prior restaurant experience. His path to tonkatsu began serendipitously: a friend took him to a cele

chef_bio.background: Takano spent decades working for a railway company, commuting between Tokyo and Nagano. At age 50 he took early retirement due to health concerns from years of shift work, with no prior restaurant experience. His path to tonkatsu began serendipitously: a friend took him to a celebrated tonkatsu restaurant as a retirement gift, where the chef taught him the fundamentals of frying meat.
chef_bio.philosophy: Takano challenges the prevailing tonkatsu orthodoxy: he believes the flavor of pork peaks around 67°C internally, so he treats the frying process as a two-stage technique borrowed from teppanyaki. He sources four premium Shinshu pork varieties — SPF, Chiyo Phantom, Azumino pasture-raised, and Yamato — and presents a whole block of meat tableside so guests choose their own cut.
chef_bio.specialty: Low-temperature-rested tonkatsu — the cutlet is briefly seared at high heat then held at 65–70°C for up to 15 minutes, letting residual heat bring the interior to its optimal 67°C. The result is a thin, shatteringly crisp crust over meat that is juicy, tender, and lighter than conventional deep-frying.

anecdotes.text: Jomando operates out of a 100-year-old renovated boarding house that doubles as a used bookstore and retro café called Daifukuya (大福屋). The restaurant sign only goes up during lunch hours; outside of that window, the space reverts to its bookshop identity.

Takano spent years refining the resting time after frying — starting at 4 minutes and eventually extending to 15 — treating each order as an experiment to find the precise window that maximises umami without drying the meat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All tonkatsu is coated in panko (wheat breadcrumbs) and fried in shared oil. No source documents a dedicated GF fryer or any separation of wheat-free items.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: House-made dipping sauce is served alongside rock salt. The soy sauce base of the house sauce almost certainly contains wheat; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes involve pork; miso soup is pork-broth based. No plant-only cooking surface or preparation area is described.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy protocol, allergen menu, or staff allergy-handling practice is documented in any reviewed source. Guests with dietary restrictions should call the restaurant directly before reserving.

positives.text: The reservation-only format means each order is prepared individually to order, which in principle reduces the risk of rushed cross-contamination compared to high-volume kitchens.

cultural_comfort.note: Owner greets reservation guests by name; all service is in Japanese, but the reservation-only format means you communicate by phone in advance — some English may be manageable with patience.

places.name: TIKU－（チクー） (TIKU-)
places.gf_detail: Wheat is the foundation of the entire concept: dough is made from a blend of Hokkaido and Nagano domestic wheat flour, natural yeast, and sea salt — baked in a wood-fired oven. There is no gluten-free alternative bread or pizza base mentioned in any source. Cross-contamination risk is high. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is omnivore-oriented — signature pizzas use cheese and the sandwich lineup features prosciutto, bacon, and salami. Some vegetable-heavy sandwiches may exist but hidden dairy (cheese) is standard. No plant-based or vegan designation noted anywhere. Ask specifically, and expect limited options at best.
places.hours_raw: Eat-in 11:00–15:00, Takeout 10:30–15:00; closes when sold out. Irregular closed days — check Instagram (@tiku_nagano) before visiting. Monday and Tuesday typically closed.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in March 2012 by a husband-and-wife team in a renovated traditional machiya (folk house) a short walk from Zenkoji Temple. After nearly a decade as a beloved wood-fired pizza restaurant, the couple closed the pizza format in November 2021 — citing a deliberate decision to

chef_bio.background: Opened in March 2012 by a husband-and-wife team in a renovated traditional machiya (folk house) a short walk from Zenkoji Temple. After nearly a decade as a beloved wood-fired pizza restaurant, the couple closed the pizza format in November 2021 — citing a deliberate decision to sustain the business long-term — then reopened in 2022 as a takeout-only wood-fired bread sandwich shop. By 2024–2025 the shop had evolved again into TIKU-Plus, blending sit-down wood-fired pizza with the sandwich counter under the same roof.
chef_bio.philosophy: Quality ingredients above all: natural sea salt, pesticide-free vegetables, and long-fermented dough baked in a wood-fired oven. The owners' handwritten menu and personal touches in service reflect the couple's genuine care for every plate.
chef_bio.specialty: Wood-fired pizza and bread sandwiches made with domestic wheat, natural yeast, and natural sea salt; topped with organically grown or pesticide-free vegetables and artisan cheeses sourced from Okayama, Hokkaido, and Nagano.

anecdotes.text: The shop is perpetually fully booked — early visits or advance reservations are essential, as items regularly sell out by midday.
After closing the original pizza format in late 2021, the owners offered weekend-only takeout pizza to keep regulars happy during the transition period.
The latest iteration, TIKU-Plus, has brought back sit-down wood-fired pizza alongside the sandwich counter, offering a tomato-cheese-basil pizza for 1,550 yen in a warm machiya setting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat flour is the core ingredient of every item on the menu — pizza dough, bread rolls, and sandwich bread are all made with domestic wheat. No gluten-free alternatives exist. Cross-contamination throughout the entire kitchen is certain.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce reported in this Italian/pizza format, but wheat is omnipresent in the bread and dough base. Wheat avoidance is not possible here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cheese is standard on pizzas and in many sandwiches; prosciutto, bacon, and salami are common sandwich fillings. Dairy cross-contact is unavoidable in this kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergy card, or staff allergy-handling protocol has been reported in any source. Communication is entirely in Japanese; allergy requests should be made by phone in advance.

positives.text: Ingredients are carefully sourced: organically grown or pesticide-free vegetables, artisan cheeses from small producers in Okayama, Hokkaido, and Nagano, and dough made from domestic wheat with natural yeast and natural sea salt — a clean, additive-free kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: Small husband-and-wife spot in a traditional machiya; no English signage reported, but the self-service cafeteria-style ordering and a simple visual menu make ordering manageable even without Japanese.

places.name: 玉照院 (Gyokushoin)
places.gf_detail: Shojin ryori avoids meat and fish but commonly uses wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) in seasoning, and some dishes may include wheat-based fu (wheat gluten cake), which is a traditional shojin ingredient. No dedicated gluten-free menu or celiac-safe protocol has been confirmed for Gyokushoin. Italian-fusion line (THE SHOJIN) may introduce pasta or wheat components. Guests with celiac disease should call ahead (026-232-2546) to ask specifically about shoyu, fu, and cross-contamination before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: Traditional shojin ryori is fully plant-based by design — no meat, fish, or animal products, in accordance with Buddhist precepts. The Italian-fusion line (THE SHOJIN) likewise follows shojin principles. No hidden dashi from katsuo or bonito; shojin dashi uses kombu and shiitake. No specific staff confirmation on record, but the cuisine category guarantees animal-free by definition.
places.hours_raw: Meals tied to lodging (2 meals included with overnight stay); day-visitor shojin lunch by reservation only (minimum 5 people, reserve at least 1 week in advance). No regular walk-in restaurant hours published.
places.neighborhood: Moto-Zenko-cho (元善町), within Zenkoji temple grounds
places.notes: Gyokushoin (玉照院) is one of the 39 permanent temple lodges (永代宿坊) on the grounds of Zenkoji, the celebrated non-sectarian Buddhist temple in Nagano that has drawn pilgrims for over 1,400 years. The lodge operates as an experience-oriented shukubo, blending traditional temple hospi

chef_bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture, Japan — within the precincts of Zenkoji temple
chef_bio.background: Gyokushoin (玉照院) is one of the 39 permanent temple lodges (永代宿坊) on the grounds of Zenkoji, the celebrated non-sectarian Buddhist temple in Nagano that has drawn pilgrims for over 1,400 years. The lodge operates as an experience-oriented shukubo, blending traditional temple hospitality with hands-on Buddhist cultural programming for individuals, families, and corporate groups.
chef_bio.philosophy: Gyokushoin frames the temple meal as an act of mindfulness and gratitude rather than simple dining, positioning shojin ryori alongside zazen, sutra copying, and rosary-making as a path to spiritual renewal.
chef_bio.specialty: Two styles of shojin ryori: a classical Japanese set featuring bamboo-leaf sushi, sesame tofu, yuba, and vegetarian grilled dishes; and THE SHOJIN, an Italian-inflected plant-based menu that uses seasonal produce with balsamic vinegar and organic preparations.

anecdotes.text: Corporate teams, hospital staff, walking clubs, and sports teams have all visited Gyokushoin for shojin ryori experiences combined with zazen and other Buddhist practices, indicating the temple's breadth as a retreat destination beyond traditional pilgrimage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination protocols has been published. The kitchen serves a traditional temple setting and no celiac-safe procedures have been confirmed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese shojin ryori typically uses wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu) for seasoning. Additionally, fu (wheat gluten cake) is a common shojin ingredient. Neither tamari nor gluten-free shoyu alternatives have been confirmed for this kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Shojin ryori is vegan by religious design — the Buddhist precepts governing the cuisine exclude all animal products from the kitchen, making cross-contact with meat or fish highly unlikely.

staff_allergy_handling.text: English-speaking staff are noted as available, which means allergy communication is possible, but no documented allergy protocol or allergen menu exists. Direct phone inquiry (026-232-2546) before visiting is strongly recommended for anyone with gluten sensitivity or celiac disease.

positives.text: Cuisine is fully plant-based by Buddhist religious mandate — no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy, making it reliably vegan without need to interrogate individual dishes.
Day-visitor shojin lunch is available (minimum 5 people, reservation required at least 1 week ahead) at approximately ¥1,500 per person, making it accessible outside of overnight stays.

cultural_comfort.note: English-speaking staff are available according to the Nagano CVB listing, making communication feasible for foreign visitors, though the experience is rooted in Japanese Buddhist temple culture.

places.name: 善光寺宿坊 常住院 (Zenkoji Shukubo Jojuin)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is publicly documented for Jojuin. Standard shojin ryori relies heavily on soy sauce (which contains wheat) and wheat-based fu (gluten cake), making celiac-unsafe preparation the default. The zenkoji.jp listing notes that dietary accommodations for allergies and religious needs are possible upon request — making direct inquiry essential before visiting. Dedicated gluten-free preparation is not confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: Shojin ryori is inherently plant-based (no meat, fish, or pungent alliums per Buddhist precepts). However, a non-shojin kaiseki option is also available, and even the shojin course may include dashi from plant sources (kombu/shiitake) rather than bonito — this is standard shojin practice but should be confirmed at time of reservation. Hidden animal-derived ingredients are uncommon in authentic shojin but cannot be ruled out without direct inquiry.
places.hours_raw: Meal seatings by reservation only; no fixed open hours for walk-in dining. Zenkoji.jp and Rurubu list the hours as "to be discussed at the time of booking." HotPepper lists 09:00–17:00 as a general window.
places.notes: Jojuin has served as the permanent resident lodging temple (永代宿坊) of Zenkoji for approximately 1,400 years, with its principal deity being Dainichi Nyorai (Vairocana Buddha). The temple underwent major renovations in the Heisei era, creating a clean and modern interior while reta

roles: Shojin ryori dining for non-staying guests (by reservation)
chef_bio.background: Jojuin has served as the permanent resident lodging temple (永代宿坊) of Zenkoji for approximately 1,400 years, with its principal deity being Dainichi Nyorai (Vairocana Buddha). The temple underwent major renovations in the Heisei era, creating a clean and modern interior while retaining its sacred role within the Zenkoji complex. Jojuin is one of 39 sub-temple lodgings within the Zenkoji grounds, each with its own resident priest and chapel.
chef_bio.philosophy: Food is prepared without meat, fish, or pungent alliums (onion, garlic, leek, chive, shallot) in accordance with Buddhist precepts. The resident priest reportedly cultivates pesticide-free vegetables that appear on the table, and the kitchen emphasizes showcasing the natural flavor and umami of each seasonal ingredient with minimal manipulation.
chef_bio.specialty: Shojin kaiseki multi-course meals featuring seasonal Shinshu vegetables, handmade sesame tofu (goma dofu), and the signature 'ながの御穀膳' (Nagano grain set meal) that honors traditional grains such as millet, barley, and buckwheat.

anecdotes.text: The head priest of Jojuin personally grows pesticide-free vegetables that are incorporated into the shojin meals, reflecting the temple's commitment to grounding its cuisine in the soil and seasons of Shinshu (Nagano).

Meals are served with handcrafted original Japanese sweets (創作和菓子) and matcha tea, a traditional close to the kaiseki experience that reflects Jojuin's position as a place of hospitality as much as practice.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination protocols is publicly available. The kitchen serves both shojin and non-shojin (non-vegetarian) courses, so shared kitchen equipment cannot be ruled out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard shojin ryori uses conventional Japanese soy sauce (shoyu), which contains wheat. Fu (wheat gluten cake) is a classic shojin ingredient and appears commonly in such menus. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternatives are mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is documented to serve non-vegetarian kaiseki courses alongside shojin options; shared preparation surfaces between courses cannot be confirmed as separate from available sources.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The Zenkoji official shukubo page notes that Jojuin accommodates requests related to allergies (アレルギー) and religious dietary restrictions; specifics of how these are handled in practice are not publicly documented.

positives.text: Shojin ryori is structurally free of meat, fish, shellfish, and pungent alliums by religious tradition — a strong baseline for many vegan and plant-based diners.
The resident priest cultivates pesticide-free vegetables used in the meals, suggesting high ingredient integrity and intentional sourcing.

cultural_comfort.note: All booking and communication is in Japanese; no English-language service is documented, and the dining experience follows traditional temple hospitality etiquette.

places.name: 平五郎 本店 (Patisserie HEIGORO Honten)
places.gf_detail: Wheat (小麦) is listed as an allergen in all shaved ice items (blueberry: milk/eggs/wheat/almonds; strawberry: milk/eggs/wheat/soy). Baked goods — sablés, butter cake, cube cakes — are wheat-based by nature. No gluten-free options are indicated anywhere on the menu or website. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: All signature items — butter cakes, sablés, cube pound cakes, shaved ice — contain eggs and dairy as core ingredients. The shaved ice menu explicitly lists milk and eggs as allergens. No vegan alternatives are offered or indicated on the menu or website.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri 11:00–17:00 (last order 16:00) / Sat & Sun 10:00–17:00 (last order 16:00) / closed Wed & Thu
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 平五郎 本店 is the pastry shop of THE FUJIYA GOHONJIN, a hospitality institution whose roots reach back to 1648 when it was founded as a prestigious honjin inn (official lodging for feudal lords) on the road to Zenkoji Temple. For generations, each successive proprietor inherited the

chef_bio.origin: 長野市大門町、善光寺門前
chef_bio.background: 平五郎 本店 is the pastry shop of THE FUJIYA GOHONJIN, a hospitality institution whose roots reach back to 1648 when it was founded as a prestigious honjin inn (official lodging for feudal lords) on the road to Zenkoji Temple. For generations, each successive proprietor inherited the name 'Heigoro,' and the shop carries that name forward today. The original Fujiya hosted luminaries including statesman Ito Hirobumi and enlightenment thinker Fukuzawa Yukichi; by 1925 the building had become a three-story Western-style structure, and in 1997 it received designation as a registered tangible cultural property. A major renovation in 2006 transformed the complex into THE FUJIYA GOHONJIN — a wedding and banquet venue with an Italian restaurant — and around 2012 the confectionery arm Patisserie HEIGORO opened in a neighbouring storehouse-style building on Daimon-cho, the central street leading to Zenkoji's gates.
chef_bio.philosophy: fuseki-ryuko — unchanging essence expressed through contemporary craft; inheriting the spirit of a 370-year-old establishment while creating confections that resonate with modern palates.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal shaved ice (summer, ¥1,800 with tea) alongside year-round baked goods: Hotel Fuji Butter Cake made with Okinawan hon-wakatou cane sugar, cube-shaped pound cakes (Cake au Cube) in apple/matcha/vanilla, and eight varieties of hand-crafted sablés.

anecdotes.text: The name 'Heigoro' was not invented for a brand — it is the historic title passed down by each successive owner of the Fujiya inn since the Edo period, making every product sold here a small inheritance of that centuries-old hospitality.

HEIGORO's shaved ice has become an annual summer pilgrimage for Nagano locals; one food blogger noted visiting 'every year as a tradition' while remarking that prices reflect both the temple-town location and inflation pressures.

The patisserie was conceived as an amenity shop that could eventually serve guests if the Fujiya ever resumed inn operations — meaning every purchase subtly keeps alive the dream of reviving Nagano's most historic lodging.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All shaved ice items list wheat as an allergen; the kitchen works with wheat-containing baked goods throughout. No mention of dedicated preparation areas.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Not a savoury establishment; soy sauce is not used. However wheat is a declared allergen across all shaved ice menu items.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Eggs and dairy are core to all items; no vegan-safe preparation lines exist.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Allergen information is published per item on the website (milk, eggs, wheat, almonds, soy noted for shaved ice); no information is available about how staff handle allergy requests in person.

cultural_comfort.note: Listed on Tabelog in English with English-language website content; this is a popular tourist-area patisserie near Zenkoji visited regularly by international travelers.

places.name: 横町カフェ (Yokomachi Cafe)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or labeling found across any sources. Curries likely use wheat-containing soy sauce or roux components — unconfirmed. The Nagano City tourism site lists "アレルギー対応" (allergy accommodation) as a service, suggesting staff can field allergy questions, but no celiac-safe protocol is documented. Cross-contamination risk in a shared kitchen is unknown. Ask staff directly; do not assume GF safety.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu. Staff can remove shrimp from the green curry to make it vegetarian, but the base likely contains dairy (coconut milk/butter). The spice gelato has been noted as egg-free. Most desserts contain eggs. Dashi-based broths are not confirmed; curry bases include butter chicken and beef, so the only plausible animal-free option is the gelato or selected drinks. No "vegan" labeling on menu.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–17:00 (last order 16:30), open daily
places.notes: Yokomachi Cafe is operated by Yawataya Isogoro, one of Japan's three great shichimi togarashi (seven-spice blend) makers, whose history in Nagano dates to 1736. The cafe opened around 2014 inside a striking red-brick building at the foot of the Zenkoji approach, with the design i

chef_bio.origin: 長野市横町（善光寺門前）
chef_bio.background: Yokomachi Cafe is operated by Yawataya Isogoro, one of Japan's three great shichimi togarashi (seven-spice blend) makers, whose history in Nagano dates to 1736. The cafe opened around 2014 inside a striking red-brick building at the foot of the Zenkoji approach, with the design inspired by the historic library building of Shinshu University — a nod to the founder's childhood memories. It underwent a partial renovation in spring 2022.
chef_bio.philosophy: Farm to table with a spice philosophy: viewing shichimi togarashi not just as a Japanese condiment but as a Western-style spice to be creatively applied across savory and sweet dishes, using Shinshu regional produce and self-grown ingredients wherever possible.
chef_bio.specialty: Spice-forward curry in three styles — black curry with braised Shinshu beef and roasted garam masala, red butter chicken curry, and green curry with shrimp and yuzu — alongside spice gelato made from ingredients grown on the company's own farm in Mure.

anecdotes.text: The cafe celebrated its 10th anniversary in December 2024 with a limited curry featuring 10 spices including coriander, turmeric, cardamom, clove, and fenugreek, alongside Nozawana greens dressed with house seven-spice.
Every table is stocked with eight varieties of the house spice blends, available for free tasting — an unusual touch that doubles as a hands-on product introduction.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information found about dedicated fryers or GF cross-contamination protocols. Kitchen setup is unknown.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce usage in curries or marinades is not documented in any source reviewed. Wheat content of curry bases is unconfirmed — standard Japanese curry roux typically contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No dedicated vegan preparation area is noted. The kitchen handles meat-based curries (beef, butter chicken, shrimp) alongside any plant-based options, so cross-contact is likely.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The Nagano City official tourism listing notes 'アレルギー対応' (allergy accommodation) as an available service, suggesting staff will engage with allergy questions. Direct inquiry at the counter is advised.

positives.text: Staff confirmed they can remove shrimp from the green curry upon request, showing some willingness to modify dishes for dietary needs.

cultural_comfort.note: Popular tourist-area cafe near Zenkoji with card payments and QR pay; international visitors are common, but menus and staff communication are primarily in Japanese.

places.name: マルベリーデリカテッセンアンドカフェ (Mulberry Delicatessen and Cafe)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free labeling found on the menu or website. Bread and quiche are central offerings, and pasta is a regular feature — wheat is pervasive. Some deli sides (e.g., ratatouille, vegetable escabeche) may be naturally wheat-free, but cross-contamination risk in a busy deli kitchen is unassessed and no dedicated GF protocols are mentioned. The house-made focaccia uses soy milk but still contains wheat. Celiac diners should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; this is not a GF-safe venue by default.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetable-forward deli sides (ratatouille, broccoli with garlic oil, purple cabbage slaw) are commonly vegan, and the shop stocks vegan products including vegan chocolate. A tofu-and-lotus-root okara hamburger retort meal is listed as a vegan option. However, the core menu is omnivore (game meats, smoked salmon pasta, quiche) and individual dishes are not systematically labeled; hidden dairy or egg in deli items is plausible. Ask staff before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Mon & Thu–Sun 11:00–22:00 (last order 21:00), Tue 11:00–18:00 (last order 17:00), closed Wed
places.notes: Mulberry Delicatessen and Cafe opened in July 2017 in a beautifully renovated former furniture store on Showa-dori, a short walk from Kencho-mae. The chef-owner trained in Canada and New York before returning to Nagano, where they previously cooked at a prestigious city hotel. Th

chef_bio.origin: 長野市大字鶴賀上千歳町 (Tsuruiga Kamisenji-cho), Nagano City; opened July 2017.
chef_bio.background: Mulberry Delicatessen and Cafe opened in July 2017 in a beautifully renovated former furniture store on Showa-dori, a short walk from Kencho-mae. The chef-owner trained in Canada and New York before returning to Nagano, where they previously cooked at a prestigious city hotel. The two-floor space — soaring ceilings, warm wood, large windows — feels nothing like a typical Nagano lunch spot.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen champions Shinshu terroir and sustainable eating: game meats (venison, wild boar) sourced from local hunters are offered as both a culinary and ecological statement, reducing crop damage while supplying the menu. Vegetable-forward sides and house-made breads using whole and mixed grains round out a quietly health-conscious approach.
chef_bio.specialty: Customizable deli-plate lunch: diners choose from a color-coded showcase of prepared dishes — ratatouille, escabeche, venison curry, quiche — assembled into a single plate. The rotating weekly specials lean heavily on local Shinshu produce and foraged game meat.

anecdotes.text: A blogger visiting in September 2017 noted the house-made focaccia used soy milk instead of dairy, and praised the mixed-grain bread options alongside the deli counter — an early signal of the kitchen's interest in ingredient substitutions.
The shop posts a 'babies crying is welcome' sign, and the ground-floor counter doubles as a takeout window — regulars treat it as a neighborhood deli as much as a sit-down restaurant.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated GF kitchen or fryer is mentioned. The deli prepares quiche, pasta, and bread alongside vegetable sides in the same space; cross-contamination risk for celiac diners is unquantified.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information on soy sauce usage in dressings or marinades. Japanese-inflected dishes on the rotating menu (gapao, green curry, game-meat preparations) may use soy sauce; no labeling is provided.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan-friendly sides are served alongside meat, fish, and dairy dishes from the same showcase counter; shared serving utensils and proximity to non-vegan items are plausible concerns.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No documented allergy protocol found. Reservations can be made via LINE or email (mulberry714@icloud.com), which are reasonable channels to communicate dietary needs in advance.

positives.text: A number of deli sides — ratatouille, broccoli with garlic oil, vegetable escabeche, purple cabbage slaw — are naturally plant-based and likely wheat-free as standalone dishes.
The restaurant stocks vegan chocolate (CoCo ChouChou brand) and a vegan tofu-lotus root okara hamburger retort, signaling openness to plant-based eating.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant has an English name, maintains an English-friendly web presence, and Tabelog lists it in English; staff are accustomed to non-Japanese visitors.

places.name: カフェ トケトケ (Cafe Toque Toque)
places.gf_detail: The main menu items, pasta (fresh noodles) and pancakes, both have wheat flour as their primary ingredient. No information on gluten-free menu items or dedicated cooking equipment could be confirmed. Soy sauce use is unclear. Celiacs should always confirm with the shop before visiting. NOT celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Pasta (cream-sauce style) and pancakes (butter, maple) are the main menu items. They are likely to contain a lot of animal-derived ingredients, and no information on vegan menu items could be confirmed. Confirm before visiting. Not confirmed vegan.
places.hours_raw: Wed-Sun 11:30-16:00 (lunch last order 13:30, pancakes from 14:00). Closed Mon and Tue.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A hideaway cafe on the west side of Zenkoji, adjacent to Yufuku Shrine, converted from a 60-70-year-old traditional house (kominka). 'toque toque' is said to mean 'resonance' or 'brushstroke' in Spanish.

chef_bio.background: 善光寺の西側、裕福神社に隣接する築60〜70年の古民家を改装した隠れ家的カフェ。 「toque toque」はスペイン語で「響き」や「筆づかい」を意味するとされる。
chef_bio.philosophy: 靴を脱いでスリッパに履き替えて入るスタイルが示すように、おうちのような温かみを大切にした空間づくり。 季節ごとにかき氷など限定メニューを加え、地域の常連客に愛されている。
chef_bio.specialty: 銅板で一枚ずつ丁寧に焼き上げる昔ながらのふわふわパンケーキ（バター・メープルシロップ添え）。 ランチタイムにはフレッシュパスタのランチセットも提供。

anecdotes.text: 古民家の趣を活かした猫モチーフのインテリアと、温かい照明が「おばあちゃんの家」のような懐かしさを演出すると複数のブロガーが紹介している。
パンケーキは夏季に提供休止となり、代わりにケニア産無農薬紅茶シロップを使ったかき氷が登場する。

cultural_comfort.note: 小規模な古民家カフェで英語メニューや英語対応の記録はなく、日本語のみの環境と思われる。

places.name: しふぉん＆ケーキ 菓恋 (Shifon & Cake Karen)
places.gf_detail: Chiffon cake is a wheat-flour-based product by definition. No gluten-free options or dedicated GF preparations mentioned anywhere. Celiac and wheat-allergic diners should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: Standard chiffon cakes are made with eggs, dairy (butter, cream), and wheat flour. No vegan menu items or substitutions mentioned across any sources. The strawberry parfait features whipped cream. Vegetable-flavored chiffon varieties exist but are not egg-free or dairy-free.
places.hours_raw: Wed–Sun 11:00–17:00 (closes once sold out), closed Mon, Tue & Fri (varies on holidays and event days)
places.neighborhood: 東町 (Higashimachi), near Zenkoji Temple eastern approach
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A compact chiffon cake specialty shop and cafe housed in a renovated 100-year-old traditional wooden machiya near the eastern approach to Zenkoji Temple. The proprietor, whose name is not publicly documented, opened the shop out of a deep personal love for sweets — a passion refl

chef_bio.background: A compact chiffon cake specialty shop and cafe housed in a renovated 100-year-old traditional wooden machiya near the eastern approach to Zenkoji Temple. The proprietor, whose name is not publicly documented, opened the shop out of a deep personal love for sweets — a passion reflected in the name 菓恋 (Karen), which can be read as 'love of confections. '
chef_bio.philosophy: Handmade, additive-free sweets using carefully selected seasonal and local ingredients. The shop closes when daily cakes sell out — freshness over volume.
chef_bio.specialty: Fluffy, moist chiffon cakes made with free-range eggs; 30+ varieties on rotation with 6–8 available daily, including vegetable and Japanese-ingredient-inspired flavors. The seasonal strawberry parfait (December–May), using Nakano City strawberries sourced directly from farmers, is a signature draw.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name 菓恋 is a play on the Japanese word for 'cute' (可愛い, kawaii) and means literally 'love of sweets' — reflecting the proprietor's stated motivation: wanting to see the happy faces of customers eating delicious things.
The building is over 100 years old — a renovated traditional wooden house steps from Zenkoji's eastern gate, giving the interior a distinctive retro-modern atmosphere.

cultural_comfort.note: Tiny 8-seat local cafe near Zenkoji with no English menu mentioned; some tourist foot traffic from the temple area may mean basic communication is possible, but this is a Japanese-language environment.

places.name: アンブロジア (ambrosia)
places.gf_detail: A wheat-containing pasta course is a standard component of the set menu, and Italian sauces and bread are served throughout. No gluten-free menu or allergen accommodation is publicly advertised. Cross-contamination risk in a small open kitchen is a realistic concern. Inquire at the time of reservation — the very small team and advance-booking format may allow customisation, but celiac diners should confirm explicitly.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is a multi-course chef's-choice set (lunch and dinner) featuring fish, meat (including Agu pork), housemade bread, and pasta. No vegan menu or substitution option is publicly advertised. Hidden animal products (dashi, butter, eggs) are present throughout. Inquire at reservation time whether any plant-based accommodation is possible given the chef-driven format.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:30, Dinner 17:30–21:00; closed Wed, Thu, 1st and 3rd Tue (plus irregular holidays). Fully reservation-only for both lunch and dinner since February 2020.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened January 2019 in a quietly renovated space behind Zenkoji Temple. Chef Yuji Nagaoka trained in Italy and honed his craft at the long-established Nagano hotel restaurant THE FUJIYA GOHONJIN before striking out independently. His wife Kana, who handles all the pastry work, pr

chef_bio.chef_name: Nagaoka Yuji (長岡勇士) — chef/owner; Nagaoka Kana (長岡佳奈) — pastry chef/owner
chef_bio.background: Opened January 2019 in a quietly renovated space behind Zenkoji Temple. Chef Yuji Nagaoka trained in Italy and honed his craft at the long-established Nagano hotel restaurant THE FUJIYA GOHONJIN before striking out independently. His wife Kana, who handles all the pastry work, previously worked at Nagano patisserie HEIGORO.
chef_bio.philosophy: Tiny-scale, ingredient-led cooking: a husband-and-wife team of eight seats in a herringbone-floored converted farmhouse, entirely reservation-only, where the menu follows the season and the chef's own catch.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal Italian chef's-choice course menus built around locally sourced Shinshu vegetables, hand-caught fish (the chef fishes himself, serving yellowtail in winter and red seabream and squid in summer), and regional Italian preparations such as Catania-style caponata with swordtip squid. The pasticceria counter offers European baked goods — galette bretonne, domestic-lemon cake, cream puffs with Tahitian vanilla custard — for eat-in or takeaway.

anecdotes.text: The fish on any given course may literally be what the chef caught himself — in summer, red seabream and squid; in winter, yellowtail from local waters.
Since February 2020, even cut and whole cakes from the pasticceria counter require advance reservation — a sign of just how small-scale and intentional the operation is.

cultural_comfort.note: Entirely reservation-based, reservation system is in Japanese (Toreta), and the restaurant is a quiet neighborhood spot with no indication of English-language service — some Japanese ability or a Japanese-speaking companion will be very helpful.

places.name: お昼ごはんとおやつ カフェ日々 (Cafe Hibi (Ohiru-gohan to Oyatsu Cafe Hibi))
places.gf_detail: The kitchen's signature is natural-yeast bread made from domestic wheat — gluten is central to the menu. No gluten-free alternatives or dedicated prep areas are mentioned in any source. Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) likely used in savoury items. Not suitable for celiac diners without direct confirmation; call ahead on 026-232-0239.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centres on handmade natural-yeast bread, homemade custard pudding (contains eggs and dairy), curry sets, and seasonal desserts. The cafe emphasises "body-friendly" ingredients and uses organic teas and domestic wheat, but no explicitly vegan dishes or labelling are documented in any source. Dairy and eggs appear throughout. Ask before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Wed, Thu & Sat 11:30–17:00 (last order 16:00); closed Sun, Mon, Tue, Fri & holidays
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Cafe Hibi opened around 2011 in a quiet back-alley in Nagano's Suwa-cho neighbourhood, midway between Zenkoji Temple and Nagano Station. The owner bakes bread from scratch each morning using domestic wheat flour and Hoshino natural yeast — the same traditional starter used in Jap

chef_bio.origin: 長野市諏訪町, 長野県
chef_bio.background: Cafe Hibi opened around 2011 in a quiet back-alley in Nagano's Suwa-cho neighbourhood, midway between Zenkoji Temple and Nagano Station. The owner bakes bread from scratch each morning using domestic wheat flour and Hoshino natural yeast — the same traditional starter used in Japanese artisan baking. The blue door is the only landmark; the interior holds just ten seats and is styled in a shabby-chic palette of white, pale blue, and warm wood.
chef_bio.philosophy: Wholesome, handcrafted food made with 'body-friendly' ingredients — seasonal produce, natural fermentation, organic teas — served at an unhurried pace in a space that feels like a friend's living room.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade custard pudding and natural-yeast bread plates; daily menu rotates with seasonal fruit and vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is so small and popular that regular visitors recommend reserving via Instagram DM before making the trip, as unannounced closures are common.
Lifestyle magazine 暮らしの手帖 is kept on the counter — a quiet signal of the aesthetic the place cultivates.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination controls is available from any source. Bread baking (wheat flour) is the kitchen's core daily activity, making airborne flour a realistic concern for highly sensitive individuals.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is likely used in savoury dishes such as curry; no tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy and eggs are used throughout the menu (custard pudding, bread, cakes); shared prep surfaces are probable in this very small kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling procedures are mentioned in any reviewed source. The cafe can be contacted by phone on 026-232-0239 to ask directly.

positives.text: Uses domestic wheat and Hoshino natural yeast; offers organic leaf teas and caffeine-free rooibos — some ingredient mindfulness is present even if allergen labelling is absent.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, unhurried neighbourhood cafe near Zenkoji with no English signage; Japanese helps for ordering, but a relaxed atmosphere makes it approachable even for non-speakers who can point at items.

places.name: 喫茶Sirafu (Kissa Sirafu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options advertised. Toast sets and chimaki involve wheat; standard Japanese soy sauce likely used. No dedicated gluten-free protocol or kitchen separation confirmed. Celiac diners should ask in advance and treat this as a conventional kissaten.
places.vegan_detail: No explicitly vegan dishes confirmed. The menu centers on morning sets (toast, Chinese chimaki sticky rice with pork, eggs), cakes, coffee, and a genmai (brown rice) latte. Hidden animal ingredients are likely in most dishes; ask on arrival.
places.hours_raw: Wed–Fri 8:00–15:00, Sat 8:00–16:00, closed Sun, Mon & Tue (hours may vary; confirmation advised)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 喫茶Sirafu opened in April 2022 in a renovated two-story traditional townhouse in Nagano's Yokosawa-cho, directly across from Yufuku Shrine and a short walk from Zenkoji Temple. The owner — a music enthusiast — curates the BGM with care, pairing a Toshiba CD player fed through Bose

chef_bio.background: 喫茶Sirafu opened in April 2022 in a renovated two-story traditional townhouse in Nagano's Yokosawa-cho, directly across from Yufuku Shrine and a short walk from Zenkoji Temple. The owner — a music enthusiast — curates the BGM with care, pairing a Toshiba CD player fed through Bose Bluetooth speakers with records such as Judee Sill's Heart Food. The first floor doubles as a browsing nook stocked with manga and curated miscellany; the second floor is the main cafe space, with large windows flooding the room with light and framing seasonal views of the shrine torii.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 'Sirafu' (しらふ) — meaning 'sober' or 'as you are' — signals the owner's invitation: come as yourself, no pretense needed. The cafe describes itself as a morning-focused spot uncommon in Nagano, emphasizing a slow, meditative atmosphere over speed.
chef_bio.specialty: 中華ちまきセット（もち米と豚の角煮）、レモンケーキ、のぶしな玄米ラテ

anecdotes.text: A music writer visiting for a café-music essay noted that the owner selects BGM 'with an emphasis on comfort,' and that the interplay of Judee Sill playing softly while leaves rustled outside created a meditative, present-moment quality rare in city cafes.

The chimaki morning set — sticky rice with braised pork, boiled egg, and pickles — was flagged by multiple bloggers as genuinely unusual for a Nagano cafe morning menu, setting Sirafu apart from standard toast-only kissaten.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, quiet neighborhood kissaten with no English menu; basic Japanese for ordering is helpful, but the relaxed, welcoming atmosphere makes non-Japanese speakers feel at ease.

places.name: 大福とウイスキーの店 いづみや民藏 (Izumiya Tamizo — Daifuku and Whiskey Bar)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free labeling or claims. The bar has evolved its food offering and now serves items including pizza, sausage, and oden — all of which typically contain gluten or wheat-based soy sauce. Daifuku mochi (if available) is made from glutinous rice and is traditionally wheat-free, but cross-contamination risk in a small bar kitchen is unknown. Pure whiskey on the rocks is the safest choice for celiac diners; food accompaniments should be treated with caution and queried individually.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling. The bar's food accompaniments have evolved from daifuku mochi to items like smoked nuts, pizza, sausage, and oden — most of which contain animal products. Daifuku, if still served, typically contains dairy-free red bean paste but the mochi skin powder coating may vary. Ask the master what is available on the night; smoked nuts may be the safest plant-based option.
places.hours_raw: 19:30–22:30, closed Thu & Sun, plus occasional irregular closures
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The building has housed the Shimizu family's hospitality business since the Taisho era, when founding patriarch Shimizu Minzo (清水民蔵) opened it as a ryokan. In 1950 the inn was renamed いづみや旅館 and continued to operate with a folk-arts café space on the ground floor. After the lodgi

chef_bio.origin: 長野市元善町 / 善光寺仲見世通り
chef_bio.background: The building has housed the Shimizu family's hospitality business since the Taisho era, when founding patriarch Shimizu Minzo (清水民蔵) opened it as a ryokan. In 1950 the inn was renamed いづみや旅館 and continued to operate with a folk-arts café space on the ground floor. After the lodging business wound down, fifth-generation owner Shimizu Akira — who brings 30 years of professional Japanese-cuisine training — renovated the tea room and kitchen into a hidden whiskey bar, reopening in April 2022 under the original founder's name.
chef_bio.philosophy: Shimizu describes the concept as an entirely new way to appreciate whiskey — pairing Japan's distilled spirit tradition with the textures and flavours of Japanese confectionery, in a space that carries the weight of a century of Nagano hospitality.
chef_bio.specialty: A poured whiskey with daifuku mochi as the otoshi (house appetizer); the bar stocks roughly 60 labels spanning bourbon, single malt, blended, and Japanese whisky, all priced the same whether taken neat, on the rocks, with water, or with soda.

anecdotes.text: The original founder's name, 民蔵 (Minzo), gives the bar its name — a deliberate act of return to origins (原点回帰) after three generations of operating as a conventional inn.
In winter the traditional irori hearth is lit, letting guests warm themselves by the fire while nursing a dram — a detail Shimizu preserved from the ryokan's folk-arts café era.
The first thing placed in front of every guest is a daifuku — a pillow of mochi filled with red bean paste — alongside the first pour of whiskey; guests may take the daifuku home if they prefer.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available on kitchen cross-contamination practices; the bar operates out of a small renovated kitchen space with no GF protocols documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Food items such as oden typically rely on soy sauce (which contains wheat) in the broth; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Sausage and other meat-based snacks are served in the same small bar space; vegan cross-contact risk from shared preparation surfaces is unknown.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling procedures or staff training information is documented for this venue; the master operates largely alone at a 13-seat bar, so direct verbal communication with him is the only practical route.

positives.text: Neat whiskey and spirits are naturally gluten-free; a primarily drinks-focused visit carries minimal dietary risk for GF drinkers who skip the food accompaniments.

cultural_comfort.note: An intimate counter bar run by a fifth-generation local family; the master is described as personable and approachable, but there is no sign of English menus or English-speaking staff — a few words of Japanese or pointing at bottles will go a long way.

places.name: 手揉み中華そば まるもも 長野店 (Temomi Chuka Soba Marumomo Nagano)
places.gf_detail: The signature hand-kneaded noodles are high-hydration wheat noodles. The tare (seasoning base) is almost certainly soy-sauce-based and contains wheat. Fried chicken (karaage) and gyoza on the menu add further wheat exposure. No allergen information posted, no GF claims anywhere. Not safe for celiac.
places.vegan_detail: All broths are animal-based — pork and chicken simmered 10+ hours. Toppings include char siu pork, flavored egg, and fried chicken. No plant-based options noted anywhere on the menu.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu 17:30-3:00 (next day), Fri-Sat 17:30-5:00 (next day), closed Sun, closed the 2nd Mon of each month. A 5% late-night surcharge applies after 22:00.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Marumomo is a joint brand created by Kawamoto Takashi, owner of the Nagano-based ramen group 'Ginya,' and Watanabe Yasuyuki, owner of the acclaimed Tokyo shop 'Chuka Soba Kibi.' Born from the two men's long relationship of trust, the brand opened its first location in Kanazawa in September 2025 and opened its second, the Nagano branch, in the Gondo Arcade in Nagano City on December 12 of the same year. The Nagano branch occupies the site formerly run by 'Kaburamura.'

chef_bio.chef_name: 河本天志 (Kawamoto Tenshi) × 渡邉保之 (Watanabe Yasuyuki)
chef_bio.origin: 金沢発祥（2025年9月）、長野店2025年12月開業
chef_bio.background: まるももは長野を拠点とするラーメングループ「吟屋」のオーナー河本天志と、東京の名店「中華そば きび」の店主渡邉保之による共同ブランド。 両者の長い信頼関係から生まれたこのブランドは、2025年9月に金沢で第1号店をオープンし、同年12月12日に長野市の権堂アーケードに第2号店として長野店を開業した。 長野店は以前「かぶら村」が営業していた場所に出店。
chef_bio.philosophy: 深夜の権堂に「一軒目の〆」や「夜中のラーメン」という文化を持ち込む。 洗練された軽やかな塩分と複層的なうまみが共存するスープを目指し、手揉み麺のもちもちとした食感とのコントラストを計算した仕上がりにこだわる。
chef_bio.specialty: 手揉み多加水麺を使ったあっさり中華そば（¥900）。 豚・鶏・野菜を10時間以上かけてじっくり炊き出した透き通った黄金色のスープに、独特の波打ちと折り目が特徴の手揉み高加水麺を合わせた一杯。

anecdotes.text: 金沢の名店「麺屋大河」グループと東京の「中華そば きび」という異なる地域の二人の名人が、長年の信頼をもとに新ブランドを立ち上げた。金沢1号店のオープンからわずか3か月で長野出店を実現するスピード展開が話題を呼んだ。
権堂アーケード内でも「ラーメンストリート」と称されるエリアに出店。かつて別の飲食店が入っていた跡地に新サインを掲げると、アーケードを行き交う人々の注目を集め、オープン前から話題になった。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hand-kneaded wheat noodles are the restaurant's defining product — wheat flour is present throughout the kitchen. No separation of prep areas noted.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce type disclosed, but standard Chinese-style ramen tare almost certainly uses wheat-containing soy sauce. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are pork- and chicken-based; there is no plant-based broth option. Lard (背脂) variants mentioned. Vegan diners have no safe option here.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site notes customers with dietary restrictions should contact the restaurant directly (要問合せ), but no structured allergen protocol or allergy-trained staff information is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: Deep in the Gondo entertainment district, late-night ramen bar atmosphere; ordering is verbal at the counter — no English menu or multilingual signage noted, but the format (point-and-order ramen shop) is navigable for Japanese-comfortable visitors.

places.name: サンデー ライフ コーヒー (Sunday Life Coffee)
places.gf_detail: Food is centered on wheat-flour-based items such as toast, hot sandwiches, and baked curry bread. No information at all on gluten-free accommodation, a dedicated cooking area, or a dedicated fryer could be confirmed. Wheat risk is low if you have drinks only, but celiacs are strongly advised to confirm by phone before visiting (090-7170-2735). NOT confirmed celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Food is centered on kissaten-style items such as toast, hot sandwiches, baked curry bread, apple pie, and pudding. The coffee jelly is confirmed to use Matsuda milk from Omachi, and there is no stated fully vegan accommodation. Drinks such as coffee, tea, and rooibos tea are likely free of animal-derived components, but confirm before visiting.
places.hours_raw: Sources vary widely. Tabelog: Mon, Sat, Sun 8:30-17:00, closed Tue-Fri. reo-gourmet: Thu 12:00-21:00, Sat-Sun 10:00-17:00, closed Mon-Wed and Fri. nagano.boooon: evening cafe on Mon and Thu. Always confirm in advance before visiting (090-7170-2735).
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner Wada Yukio was raised in Matsumoto and moved to Nagano City in early childhood. After graduating from university he moved to Tokyo to pursue an interest in interior design. After returning home he worked in construction for 6-7 years, then entered the food-and-beverage world at a hotel restaurant. He kept up a three-days-a-week lunch operation for four years by borrowing the daytime downtime of a counter bar, building up capital and regular customers before going independent in an adjacent property. He holds a management philosophy of realizing his ideals step by step.

chef_bio.background: 松本育ちで幼少期に長野市に転居したオーナーの和田幸夫氏は、大学卒業後に内装デザインへの関心を追って上京。 帰郷後は建設業に6〜7年従事し、その後ホテルレストランで飲食の世界へ。 カウンターバーの昼間の空き時間を間借りする形で週3日のランチ営業を4年間続け、資金と常連を積み上げてから隣接物件に独立。 段階的に理想を実現するという経営哲学を持つ。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「お客様にはとにかくゆっくりしていって欲しい」。 コンセプトは『Mr Sunday Trip』—世界各地からヴィンテージ雑貨を集める架空の旅人をイメージし、インテリアは自身のコレクション家具と一点物の食器で構成。
chef_bio.specialty: サンデーレトロプリン（固めのカスタードプリン）と日替わりストレートコーヒー。 コーヒーゼリーは自家製コーヒーを固め、大北地方の百年牧場・松田牛乳を添えたもの。

anecdotes.text: 元々はカウンターバーが夜だけ営業していたため、昼間の空き時間を交渉して間借り営業をスタート。4年かけて常連客と資金を積み上げ、隣の物件が空いたタイミングで現在の独立店舗を開いた。
コーヒーゼリーにシナモンや七味（八幡屋礒五郎）をかけて食べる提案に、訪問客が「意外な相性の良さ」に驚いたと報告されている。

cultural_comfort.note: カウンター一体型の極小喫茶店でオーナーとの会話が自然に生まれる空間。英語メニュー・英語対応の情報はなく、日本語での基本的なやり取りが必要。

places.name: そば処とがくし (Soba Dokoro Togakushi)
places.gf_detail: Soba noodles are buckwheat but the dipping sauce (tsuyu) uses wheat-based soy sauce, confirmed as sharp and soy-forward. Udon (100% wheat) is made and served in the same small counter kitchen, making cross-contamination near-certain. No dedicated GF preparation or awareness noted. Not suitable for celiac.
places.vegan_detail: Dipping broth is confirmed katsuobushi (bonito) based. No vegan dashi alternative noted anywhere. Udon also served, sharing the kitchen. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–13:30 / 17:00–21:00; closed Sundays and public holidays
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A tiny counter soba shop that has operated for over 47 years in the heart of Nagano's Gondo arcade, tucked into the entrance of the historic Nagano Shochiku Aioiza cinema — a Showa-era neighborhood landmark. The proprietor, an elderly man who runs the shop alone, relocated from T
